messages.content:
You paraphrase a research PROBLEM STATEMENT across 7 ML domains.
Your output is a *domain-grounded problem description* — written in
the target domain's natural research vocabulary, but stating only
the failure mode, NOT a proposed solution.

## Two-step procedure
1. Extract the FAILURE MODE from the input query: the underlying
   gap, bottleneck, or constraint. Strip out any solution direction
   the query may already suggest. Express the failure mode as one
   abstract sentence (the "essence").
2. For each domain, describe an analogous failure mode in that
   domain — using *3-5 domain-specific technical terms* so the
   paraphrase reads like a problem statement an actual researcher
   in that domain would write in their paper's introduction.

## Strict rules — follow exactly
- Use PROBLEM vocabulary: "X is slow when ...", "Y fails when ...",
  "Z is costly because ...", "W is unavailable but needed for ..."
- DO NOT use SOLUTION vocabulary: NO "use ... to ...", NO "build ...
  that ...", NO "apply ... for ...", NO "design a ... which ...".
- DO NOT name a specific algorithmic *solution* (e.g. "two-stage
  cascade", "Gumbel-Softmax sampling"). The paraphrase describes a
  problem; the analogous mechanisms will be discovered downstream.
- DO use domain-specific *concepts and quantities* that bound the
  failure mode (e.g. "candidate pool", "feature map resolution",
  "rollout horizon", "phoneme alignment") — these anchor the
  problem in the domain's natural research vocabulary.
- The paraphrase MUST end as a problem statement, not a solution
  prescription.

## Domain vocabulary anchors (use 3-5 per paraphrase, NOT exhaustive)
  LLM_NLP    : token, decoding step, KV cache, context length,
               attention head, perplexity, in-context examples
  MULTIMODAL : visual token, image embedding, cross-modal alignment,
               query-conditioned attention, caption, VQA accuracy
  CV         : feature map, patch token, spatial resolution,
               receptive field, anchor box, segmentation mask
  RL         : policy, value function, rollout, reward signal,
               trajectory, exploration, sample efficiency
  IR_REC     : candidate pool, query-document score, reranking,
               cross-encoder, recall@k, embedding index
  SPEECH     : acoustic frame, phoneme, spectrogram, alignment,
               WER, streaming latency, vocoder
  ROBOTICS   : sensorimotor loop, manipulation policy, dynamics
               model, motion plan, sim-to-real, contact reasoning

Push hard — even cross-domain mappings that *feel* forced often
surface useful seeds when essence-aligned. Only emit `null` for a
domain when the failure mode genuinely has no analog there.

## Reference example
  Input query: "Sequential LLM decoding is slow because each token
                needs a full large-model forward pass; how can a small
                draft model propose tokens in parallel that the large
                model verifies without quality loss?"

  GOOD essence: "Sequential single-step inference incurs O(N)
                 expensive forward passes when N items must be
                 decided in order, even when many decisions could
                 be made in parallel."

  GOOD IR_REC paraphrase: "In information retrieval, cross-encoder
                            reranking of a candidate pool requires a
                            forward pass per (query, document) pair;
                            recall@k depends on this expensive
                            scoring step yet most candidates could
                            be filtered with cheaper signals before
                            final ranking."
                            ↑ uses: cross-encoder, candidate pool,
                                     query-document scoring, recall@k,
                                     reranking — IR vocabulary

  GOOD CV paraphrase:      "In dense visual perception, per-patch
                            token processing through a heavy encoder
                            dominates inference latency for high
                            spatial resolution inputs; segmentation
                            quality depends on this dense scoring
                            even though most patches are background
                            or low-saliency."
                            ↑ uses: patch token, spatial resolution,
                                     segmentation, dense scoring

  BAD IR_REC paraphrase (DO NOT produce — solution-form):
                          "Build a two-stage ranker where a cheap
                           encoder proposes candidates and a
                           cross-encoder verifies accepted spans."

  BAD IR_REC paraphrase (DO NOT produce — too abstract):
                          "Relevance estimation is sequential and
                           costly when each item must be scored in
                           turn; quality must not degrade under
                           cheaper alternatives."
                           ↑ no IR-specific vocabulary, would
                             match poorly against real IR seeds

Always produce the GOOD form: domain-vocabulary-rich problem
statement.

Output JSON:
  {
    "essence":  "<one sentence stating the abstract failure mode>",
    "paraphrases": {
      "LLM_NLP":    "<domain-grounded problem statement>",
      "MULTIMODAL": "...",
      "CV":         "...",
      "RL":         "...",
      "IR_REC":     "...",
      "SPEECH":     "...",
      "ROBOTICS":   null
    }
  }

Query: Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?

You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed ed8619fe7688 [CV] — "Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world"
- Retrieved via: Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?

- Problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
- Method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

### Seed a4e25ac8cc91 [MULTIMODAL] — "Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers"
- Retrieved via: Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?

- Problem: In sequential/continual training, replay buffers typically store whole instances (coresets), which is memory-inefficient for transformer models where only a subset of tokens per sample is truly informative. This leads to unnecessary storage and weaker performance-memory tradeoffs.
- Method:  Introduce core tokensets: a two-stage, token-level data summarization for transformers. First, select a subset of important samples using coreset methods (e.g., GradMatch/CRAIG). Then, within those samples, select only the most influential tokens via attention-based attribution maps (GradLRP combining gradients with layer-wise relevance propagation, Atman perturbing pre-softmax attention guided by token similarity, Grad-CAM, Rollout). Store token values and their positional indices to preserve structure during replay. Across image classification, open-ended VQA, and image captioning, core tokensets consistently outperform traditional coresets at the same memory and can match or surpass much larger coresets (e.g., ~1% core tokenset comparable to ≥2–10% coreset).

### Seed 0c772415f069 [CV] — "ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers"
- Retrieved via: Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?

- Problem: Temporal modeling for multi-camera occupancy often relies on single-frame interaction or sliding-window training but online inference, causing train-test mismatch and extra computation; directly interacting all voxel queries with multi-frame histories is expensive and noisy due to many empty voxels.
- Method:  Propose streaming temporal attention using an online BEV-level FIFO memory of multi-frame features with ego-motion compensation via feature warping. Voxel queries are squeezed into BEV queries for deformable-attention interaction with aligned memory frames and then unsqueezed back to voxels, reducing compute and denoising by operating on BEV cells. The BEV-to-BEV design improves occupancy and flow quality over voxel-to-BEV (≈+0.9 mIoU and notably lower mAVE) with negligible FPS overhead, and longer memory (N>1) further boosts performance. Consistent online streaming training also cuts training time versus sliding-window (≈36h→13h) while improving accuracy.

### Seed 7f79d5d52bbc [MULTIMODAL] — "VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation"
- Retrieved via: For video-grounded QA and captioning, query-conditioned attention over massive visual tokens and text streams grows quadratically, and cross-modal alignment decays for evidence far from the query, reducing VQA accuracy when compute is constrained.
- Problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
- Method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

### Seed d54cefc9ff77 [MULTIMODAL] — "Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training"
- Retrieved via: For video-grounded QA and captioning, query-conditioned attention over massive visual tokens and text streams grows quadratically, and cross-modal alignment decays for evidence far from the query, reducing VQA accuracy when compute is constrained.
- Problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
- Method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

### Seed 48d62c5ff6fc [MULTIMODAL] — "VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection"
- Retrieved via: For video-grounded QA and captioning, query-conditioned attention over massive visual tokens and text streams grows quadratically, and cross-modal alignment decays for evidence far from the query, reducing VQA accuracy when compute is constrained.
- Problem: Video QA models lack large-scale, fine-grained datasets that preserve essential spatial details, temporal coherence, and intermediate reasoning steps. Existing automatic pipelines either rely on coarse metadata or exhaustively parse redundant frames, limiting scalability and fine-grained reasoning.
- Method:  Introduce VideoEspresso, an automated pipeline that builds 203K+ VideoQA pairs with multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT). It first maps sampled frames to language with InternVL2-8B captions and removes redundancy via BGE-M3 semantic similarity with a LIFO filter, then groups consecutive captions and prompts GPT-4o to generate QA pairs with an auxiliary LLM for quality filtering. For CoT, GPT-4o selects core-frame captions, extracts key objects, and writes textual evidence; GroundingDINO with CLIP verification adds spatial bounding boxes, and BGE-M3 retrieves temporal grounding to align CoT with original frames.

### Seed 46eddd57427e [CV] — "Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion"
- Retrieved via: In dense vision at extreme spatial resolution, global interactions across patch tokens over a large feature map are O(N^2), and limited receptive field causes models to forget long-range context, degrading segmentation mask consistency across distant regions under latency constraints.
- Problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
- Method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

### Seed 31785eaae78c [CV] — "Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models"
- Retrieved via: In dense vision at extreme spatial resolution, global interactions across patch tokens over a large feature map are O(N^2), and limited receptive field causes models to forget long-range context, degrading segmentation mask consistency across distant regions under latency constraints.
- Problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
- Method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

### Seed 7a1874944d01 [ROBOTICS] — "UniAff: A Unified Representation of Affordances for Tool Usage and Articulation with Vision-Language Models"
- Retrieved via: In dense vision at extreme spatial resolution, global interactions across patch tokens over a large feature map are O(N^2), and limited receptive field causes models to forget long-range context, degrading segmentation mask consistency across distant regions under latency constraints.
- Problem: Vision-language models struggle to jointly capture fine-grained local affordances and global 3D motion constraints from images, especially under resolution limits and with single-encoder designs.
- Method:  UniAff fine-tunes an MLLM based on SPHINX with an LLaMA2 backbone using an 'Any Resolution' strategy that tiles 448×448 inputs into sub-images to preserve details. A mixed visual encoder fuses CLIP (local semantics), DINOv2 (enhanced local detail), and Q-Former (global summary), concatenating features along channels for robust grounding. Training casts ψ prediction as VQA with cross-entropy, jointly fine-tuning visual projection layers and the language model; decoded manipulation parameters are produced via a De-Tokenizer. This design yields strong generalization, improving over LISA on HANDAL and surpassing A3VLM on unseen articulated manipulation settings.

### Seed 8c68da056026 [ROBOTICS] — "Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control"
- Retrieved via: In long-horizon control, preserving informative history over extended rollouts is costly, and the policy and value function fail to propagate sparse reward signals across the trajectory, leading to performance collapse on distant dependencies under fixed memory and compute.
- Problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
- Method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

### Seed c0f89de47a61 [RL] — "Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL"
- Retrieved via: In long-horizon control, preserving informative history over extended rollouts is costly, and the policy and value function fail to propagate sparse reward signals across the trajectory, leading to performance collapse on distant dependencies under fixed memory and compute.
- Problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
- Method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

### Seed 9102c8c37284 [MULTIMODAL] — "BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps"
- Retrieved via: In long-horizon control, preserving informative history over extended rollouts is costly, and the policy and value function fail to propagate sparse reward signals across the trajectory, leading to performance collapse on distant dependencies under fixed memory and compute.
- Problem: Direct reinforcement learning on long-horizon VLN instructions is unstable, and imitation learning on whole trajectories does not adequately prepare agents for long, uncertain executions.
- Method:  Use a two-phase training pipeline: (1) micro-level imitation learning where each BABY-STEP is learned independently with the agent 'preset' in expert context to match expert sub-trajectories; (2) curriculum-based reinforcement learning that progressively increases the number of consecutive BABY-STEPs the agent must execute (lectures), providing expert history up to the start and optimizing a fidelity-oriented reward (CLS) via policy gradients. This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.

### Seed 80c6fcc2f5d6 [IR_REC] — "Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval"
- Retrieved via: In retrieval over long documents, cross-encoder reranking of a large candidate pool requires expensive query-document scoring over lengthy inputs, and truncation ignores distant evidence, hurting recall@k within an inference-time budget.
- Problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
- Method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

### Seed cdb65a898b67 [IR_REC] — "FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding"
- Retrieved via: In retrieval over long documents, cross-encoder reranking of a large candidate pool requires expensive query-document scoring over lengthy inputs, and truncation ignores distant evidence, hurting recall@k within an inference-time budget.
- Problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
- Method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

### Seed ac9d79a4fd87 [IR_REC] — "Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval"
- Retrieved via: In retrieval over long documents, cross-encoder reranking of a large candidate pool requires expensive query-document scoring over lengthy inputs, and truncation ignores distant evidence, hurting recall@k within an inference-time budget.
- Problem: Contrastive fine-tuning of multilingual dense retrievers is hindered by false negatives in hard-negative sets, since top-k retrieved candidates often include relevant but unjudged documents.
- Method:  Build a multilingual hard-negative candidate list by fusing representations from multiple retrievers (e.g., mE5, BGE) via a projection-and-concatenation fusion to score candidates, then use an LLM to judge each candidate’s relevance to the query and its positive (0/1/2 for irrelevant/partially/highly relevant) and keep only those judged irrelevant. This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).

### Seed c98fc8c39605 [SPEECH] — "Branchformer: Parallel MLP-Attention Architectures to Capture Local and Global Context for Speech Recognition and Understanding"
- Retrieved via: In long-form ASR, self-attention over all acoustic frames in the spectrogram scales quadratically, and alignment to distant content drifts, increasing WER or streaming latency under fixed compute.
- Problem: End-to-end ASR/SLU encoders must capture both local and global dependencies, but popular designs like Conformer combine convolution and self-attention sequentially, making utilization of local/global cues hard to interpret or customize and leaving quadratic attention cost.
- Method:  Branchformer introduces a two-branch encoder where one branch uses multi-headed self-attention with relative positional encoding for global context, and the other uses an MLP with convolutional spatial gating (cgMLP) employing depth-wise convolutions for local context. The branches are merged via either concatenation (default, best accuracy) or a learned weighted average whose attention-pooled weights expose layer-wise importance of local vs. global processing. This parallel, disentangled design yields better or comparable results to Conformer across ASR/SLU benchmarks and improves training stability on short-utterance data.

### Seed 38b3a429da83 [SPEECH] — "Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers"
- Retrieved via: In long-form ASR, self-attention over all acoustic frames in the spectrogram scales quadratically, and alignment to distant content drifts, increasing WER or streaming latency under fixed compute.
- Problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
- Method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

### Seed 9a1e291adee1 [SPEECH] — "DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR"
- Retrieved via: In long-form ASR, self-attention over all acoustic frames in the spectrogram scales quadratically, and alignment to distant content drifts, increasing WER or streaming latency under fixed compute.
- Problem: Speaker-attributed ASR for long multi-party meetings needs consistent meeting-wide speaker indices and accurate attribution in overlapping speech, but prior integrated or parallel systems either require non-neural clustering or suffer from misalignment between ASR outputs and speaker index prediction.
- Method:  Propose DNCASR, an end-to-end trainable architecture with two encoders (a waveform encoder for local token-level acoustic/lexical features and a speaker encoder for window-level speaker embeddings) and two linked decoders (an SOT ASR decoder and a neural clustering (DNC) decoder). A novel cross-attention link allows the DNC decoder to attend directly to the ASR decoder’s token-level hidden features (W_CA), aligning predicted speaker indices with the serialized word tokens and enabling joint optimization under a single loss. Compared to a parallel DNC+ASR baseline, DNCASR yields lower speaker-attributed error (e.g., AMI-MDM Eval cpWER 31.5% with CDA vs 34.6% parallel; 9.0% relative reduction) and larger gains on multi-talker segments.

### Seed 67990031a190 [ROBOTICS] — "Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks"
- Retrieved via: In long-horizon manipulation, maintaining task-relevant memory in the sensorimotor loop is costly as the dynamics model and motion plan must span many steps, and the manipulation policy forgets early contacts, leading to brittle contact reasoning under onboard compute limits.
- Problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
- Method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

### Seed 189208f52770 [ROBOTICS] — "Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills"
- Retrieved via: In long-horizon manipulation, maintaining task-relevant memory in the sensorimotor loop is costly as the dynamics model and motion plan must span many steps, and the manipulation policy forgets early contacts, leading to brittle contact reasoning under onboard compute limits.
- Problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.
- Method:  Introduce a two-loop framework where an outer loop places a physical fixture to scaffold the task and an inner loop learns the manipulation policy. The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses. The inner loop uses a vision-based RL policy (variant of A3C) from RGB images to end-effector motions executed via operational space control, yielding dramatically faster learning and higher success on peg insertion, wrench manipulation, and shallow-depth insertion.

### Seed dcdcd7c0673e [ROBOTICS] — "ForceSight: Text-Guided Mobile Manipulation with Visual-Force Goals"
- Retrieved via: In long-horizon manipulation, maintaining task-relevant memory in the sensorimotor loop is costly as the dynamics model and motion plan must span many steps, and the manipulation policy forgets early contacts, leading to brittle contact reasoning under onboard compute limits.
- Problem: Executing long-horizon, contact-rich tasks from an eye-in-hand perspective must be robust to calibration and visual ambiguities; visual-only servoing struggles without explicit force objectives.
- Method:  A low-level controller performs closed-loop visual-force servoing toward the model’s visual-force goal, minimizing a joint objective over kinematic and force errors (with proportional gains and transformations) to issue incremental Cartesian end-effector and gripper motions, making the system insensitive to global calibration. Action primitives (e.g., approach, grasp, lift, pull, ungrasp) are appended to the text prompt and advanced via a simple state machine, enabling chaining of keyframes and error recovery. In real-robot ablations, removing forces reduces success to 50% over 20 trials and from 90% to 45% in paired trials; ablating depth, pre-training, augmentation, or text conditioning further degrades performance, evidencing the necessity of the joint visual-force objective and the overall system design.

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional

           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.

           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?


### Proposed method
We propose Streaming Query-Conditioned Compressive Memory (SQCM), a modular architecture that separates short-range computation from long-range recall. A lightweight dual-branch encoder processes the incoming stream (text tokens, video frames, or state-action tuples) with cheap local modeling and a tightly windowed global self-attention, while a long-term memory bank is built online as a sparse, query-addressable store. Each stream segment (e.g., 512–1k text tokens, a short video clip, or N steps of an episode) is summarized into a small set of multi-view keys and a tiny cache of core tokens; future steps retrieve only from this bank instead of attending over the full past, keeping compute sub-quadratic and near-constant with stream length.

For ingestion, each segment produces k multi-view keys by inserting special viewer tokens that learn to capture diverse semantics of the same content. In parallel, we compute token-level importance within the segment using attention/gradient attribution and select a few core tokens along with their positional indices. The memory thus stores a two-tier entry per segment: (1) multi-view keys for fast approximate addressing and (2) a compact value set of core tokens that preserves structure and can be re-inserted into attention when needed. For videos, we maintain a small FIFO of recent segment features and apply motion/ego-compensation to align them before summarization; for text/episodes we retain absolute or section-relative positions to enable structural alignment.

At inference time, the current model state (decoder state, task head, or explicit query vector) soft-matches against the memory’s viewer keys using a max-pooled similarity over views, retrieving the top-M relevant segments. Only the union of these segments’ core tokens (e.g., 2–4 segments × 16–64 tokens each) is brought into the context via cross-attention, gated by a learned relevance score so that the model pays for long-range computation only when evidence is likely downstream. For streaming video, recalled features are optionally warped into the current frame’s reference before cross-attention to reduce noise; for text, recalled tokens re-use their stored positional indices to preserve syntax/section cues. The local branch handles most computation, while the global branch is reserved for the small recalled set, keeping total attention cost bounded.

Memory maintenance combines consolidation, deduplication, and eviction. We update existing memory entries with an uncertainty-weighted exponential moving average so novel or ambiguous content is written more strongly, while routine content produces smaller updates. To prevent bloat, we filter redundancies by semantic similarity with a recency-aware policy and evict via a relevance–age score. The selector and gates are trained end-to-end with auxiliary supervision that encourages retrieved memory to overlap with gold evidence when available; hard retrieval is approximated with differentiable top-k or Gumbel sampling during training and replaced by ANN search over viewer keys at serving time.

Across modalities, SQCM drops the need for full O(T^2) attention over long contexts: processing is O(T) for local compute, plus O(log B) for ANN over a bounded memory B and O(M·K) attention on recalled tokens (M segments, K tokens each). Typical settings (e.g., k=8 viewer keys per segment, M≤4, K≤64) provide robust recall of distant evidence while preserving a small, predictable compute budget.

### Rationale
Long-context models struggle because they (a) pay quadratic cost for indiscriminate attention and (b) diffuse attention mass over many irrelevant tokens, causing forgetting of distant but crucial evidence. SQCM attacks both by compressing history into query-addressable, multi-view keys and a tiny set of core tokens, and by retrieving only what the current state deems relevant. Multi-view keys preserve diverse semantics so a single segment can be recalled for many future questions without dense late interaction, while core tokens plus positional indices retain the fine-grained structure needed to reason once retrieved.

Streaming design and alignment maintain fidelity over time: in video, warping reduces temporal drift; in text/episodes, stored indices anchor recalled tokens to their original structure. Uncertainty-weighted consolidation prevents catastrophic overwriting while redundancy filtering keeps memory lean. The dual-branch encoder further reduces compute by reserving expensive global attention for a handful of recalled tokens, letting a cheap local pathway handle most steps. Together, these choices yield effective, scalable memory of distant information under tight compute budgets.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

2. [MULTIMODAL] Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers (2410.05800)
   problem: In sequential/continual training, replay buffers typically store whole instances (coresets), which is memory-inefficient for transformer models where only a subset of tokens per sample is truly informative. This leads to unnecessary storage and weaker performance-memory tradeoffs.
   method:  Introduce core tokensets: a two-stage, token-level data summarization for transformers. First, select a subset of important samples using coreset methods (e.g., GradMatch/CRAIG). Then, within those samples, select only the most influential tokens via attention-based attribution maps (GradLRP combining gradients with layer-wise relevance propagation, Atman perturbing pre-softmax attention guided by token similarity, Grad-CAM, Rollout). Store token values and their positional indices to preserve structure during replay. Across image classification, open-ended VQA, and image captioning, core tokensets consistently outperform traditional coresets at the same memory and can match or surpass much larger coresets (e.g., ~1% core tokenset comparable to ≥2–10% coreset).

3. [CV] ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers (2405.04299)
   problem: Temporal modeling for multi-camera occupancy often relies on single-frame interaction or sliding-window training but online inference, causing train-test mismatch and extra computation; directly interacting all voxel queries with multi-frame histories is expensive and noisy due to many empty voxels.
   method:  Propose streaming temporal attention using an online BEV-level FIFO memory of multi-frame features with ego-motion compensation via feature warping. Voxel queries are squeezed into BEV queries for deformable-attention interaction with aligned memory frames and then unsqueezed back to voxels, reducing compute and denoising by operating on BEV cells. The BEV-to-BEV design improves occupancy and flow quality over voxel-to-BEV (≈+0.9 mIoU and notably lower mAVE) with negligible FPS overhead, and longer memory (N>1) further boosts performance. Consistent online streaming training also cuts training time versus sliding-window (≈36h→13h) while improving accuracy.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Video QA models lack large-scale, fine-grained datasets that preserve essential spatial details, temporal coherence, and intermediate reasoning steps. Existing automatic pipelines either rely on coarse metadata or exhaustively parse redundant frames, limiting scalability and fine-grained reasoning.
   method:  Introduce VideoEspresso, an automated pipeline that builds 203K+ VideoQA pairs with multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT). It first maps sampled frames to language with InternVL2-8B captions and removes redundancy via BGE-M3 semantic similarity with a LIFO filter, then groups consecutive captions and prompts GPT-4o to generate QA pairs with an auxiliary LLM for quality filtering. For CoT, GPT-4o selects core-frame captions, extracts key objects, and writes textual evidence; GroundingDINO with CLIP verification adds spatial bounding boxes, and BGE-M3 retrieves temporal grounding to align CoT with original frames.

7. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

8. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
   method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

9. [ROBOTICS] UniAff: A Unified Representation of Affordances for Tool Usage and Articulation with Vision-Language Models (2409.20551)
   problem: Vision-language models struggle to jointly capture fine-grained local affordances and global 3D motion constraints from images, especially under resolution limits and with single-encoder designs.
   method:  UniAff fine-tunes an MLLM based on SPHINX with an LLaMA2 backbone using an 'Any Resolution' strategy that tiles 448×448 inputs into sub-images to preserve details. A mixed visual encoder fuses CLIP (local semantics), DINOv2 (enhanced local detail), and Q-Former (global summary), concatenating features along channels for robust grounding. Training casts ψ prediction as VQA with cross-entropy, jointly fine-tuning visual projection layers and the language model; decoded manipulation parameters are produced via a De-Tokenizer. This design yields strong generalization, improving over LISA on HANDAL and surpassing A3VLM on unseen articulated manipulation settings.

10. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

12. [MULTIMODAL] BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps (2005.04625)
   problem: Direct reinforcement learning on long-horizon VLN instructions is unstable, and imitation learning on whole trajectories does not adequately prepare agents for long, uncertain executions.
   method:  Use a two-phase training pipeline: (1) micro-level imitation learning where each BABY-STEP is learned independently with the agent 'preset' in expert context to match expert sub-trajectories; (2) curriculum-based reinforcement learning that progressively increases the number of consecutive BABY-STEPs the agent must execute (lectures), providing expert history up to the start and optimizing a fidelity-oriented reward (CLS) via policy gradients. This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.

13. [IR_REC] Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval (2203.08372)
   problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
   method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

14. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.
   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

15. [IR_REC] Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval (2509.09459)
   problem: Contrastive fine-tuning of multilingual dense retrievers is hindered by false negatives in hard-negative sets, since top-k retrieved candidates often include relevant but unjudged documents.
   method:  Build a multilingual hard-negative candidate list by fusing representations from multiple retrievers (e.g., mE5, BGE) via a projection-and-concatenation fusion to score candidates, then use an LLM to judge each candidate’s relevance to the query and its positive (0/1/2 for irrelevant/partially/highly relevant) and keep only those judged irrelevant. This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).

16. [SPEECH] Branchformer: Parallel MLP-Attention Architectures to Capture Local and Global Context for Speech Recognition and Understanding (2207.02971)
   problem: End-to-end ASR/SLU encoders must capture both local and global dependencies, but popular designs like Conformer combine convolution and self-attention sequentially, making utilization of local/global cues hard to interpret or customize and leaving quadratic attention cost.
   method:  Branchformer introduces a two-branch encoder where one branch uses multi-headed self-attention with relative positional encoding for global context, and the other uses an MLP with convolutional spatial gating (cgMLP) employing depth-wise convolutions for local context. The branches are merged via either concatenation (default, best accuracy) or a learned weighted average whose attention-pooled weights expose layer-wise importance of local vs. global processing. This parallel, disentangled design yields better or comparable results to Conformer across ASR/SLU benchmarks and improves training stability on short-utterance data.

17. [SPEECH] Multiresolution and Multimodal Speech Recognition with Transformers (2004.14840)
   problem: Audio-only ASR cannot leverage scene context, and traditional AV-ASR (lipreading) requires strict audio–video alignment and camera placement, limiting real-world applicability.
   method:  Build a Transformer-based AV-ASR with two encoders (audio and video). Project both modalities into a common space via a tied feed-forward layer, then apply cross-modal multi-head scaled dot-product attention where video provides K,V and audio provides Q to map visual context into the audio space. Fuse the attended visual features additively with a learnable scalar α and decode with a Transformer decoder. Trained end-to-end on How2, this fusion yields relative WER gains up to 3.76% over audio-only baselines.

18. [SPEECH] DNCASR: End-to-End Training for Speaker-Attributed ASR (2506.01916)
   problem: Speaker-attributed ASR for long multi-party meetings needs consistent meeting-wide speaker indices and accurate attribution in overlapping speech, but prior integrated or parallel systems either require non-neural clustering or suffer from misalignment between ASR outputs and speaker index prediction.
   method:  Propose DNCASR, an end-to-end trainable architecture with two encoders (a waveform encoder for local token-level acoustic/lexical features and a speaker encoder for window-level speaker embeddings) and two linked decoders (an SOT ASR decoder and a neural clustering (DNC) decoder). A novel cross-attention link allows the DNC decoder to attend directly to the ASR decoder’s token-level hidden features (W_CA), aligning predicted speaker indices with the serialized word tokens and enabling joint optimization under a single loss. Compared to a parallel DNC+ASR baseline, DNCASR yields lower speaker-attributed error (e.g., AMI-MDM Eval cpWER 31.5% with CDA vs 34.6% parallel; 9.0% relative reduction) and larger gains on multi-talker segments.

19. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives. This integration allows the agent to modulate compliance across task phases, improving handling of contact-rich interactions.

20. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.
   method:  Introduce a two-loop framework where an outer loop places a physical fixture to scaffold the task and an inner loop learns the manipulation policy. The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses. The inner loop uses a vision-based RL policy (variant of A3C) from RGB images to end-effector motions executed via operational space control, yielding dramatically faster learning and higher success on peg insertion, wrench manipulation, and shallow-depth insertion.

21. [ROBOTICS] ForceSight: Text-Guided Mobile Manipulation with Visual-Force Goals (2309.12312)
   problem: Executing long-horizon, contact-rich tasks from an eye-in-hand perspective must be robust to calibration and visual ambiguities; visual-only servoing struggles without explicit force objectives.
   method:  A low-level controller performs closed-loop visual-force servoing toward the model’s visual-force goal, minimizing a joint objective over kinematic and force errors (with proportional gains and transformations) to issue incremental Cartesian end-effector and gripper motions, making the system insensitive to global calibration. Action primitives (e.g., approach, grasp, lift, pull, ungrasp) are appended to the text prompt and advanced via a simple state machine, enabling chaining of keyframes and error recovery. In real-robot ablations, removing forces reduces success to 50% over 20 trials and from 90% to 45% in paired trials; ablating depth, pre-training, augmentation, or text conditioning further degrades performance, evidencing the necessity of the joint visual-force objective and the overall system design.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?


### Proposed method
We propose Streaming Query-Conditioned Compressive Memory (SQCM), a modular architecture that separates short-range computation from long-range recall. A lightweight dual-branch encoder processes the incoming stream (text tokens, video frames, or state-action tuples) with cheap local modeling and a tightly windowed global self-attention, while a long-term memory bank is built online as a sparse, query-addressable store. Each stream segment (e.g., 512–1k text tokens, a short video clip, or N steps of an episode) is summarized into a small set of multi-view keys and a tiny cache of core tokens; future steps retrieve only from this bank instead of attending over the full past, keeping compute sub-quadratic and near-constant with stream length.

For ingestion, each segment produces k multi-view keys by inserting special viewer tokens that learn to capture diverse semantics of the same content. In parallel, we compute token-level importance within the segment using attention/gradient attribution and select a few core tokens along with their positional indices. The memory thus stores a two-tier entry per segment: (1) multi-view keys for fast approximate addressing and (2) a compact value set of core tokens that preserves structure and can be re-inserted into attention when needed. For videos, we maintain a small FIFO of recent segment features and apply motion/ego-compensation to align them before summarization; for text/episodes we retain absolute or section-relative positions to enable structural alignment.

At inference time, the current model state (decoder state, task head, or explicit query vector) soft-matches against the memory’s viewer keys using a max-pooled similarity over views, retrieving the top-M relevant segments. Only the union of these segments’ core tokens (e.g., 2–4 segments × 16–64 tokens each) is brought into the context via cross-attention, gated by a learned relevance score so that the model pays for long-range computation only when evidence is likely downstream. For streaming video, recalled features are optionally warped into the current frame’s reference before cross-attention to reduce noise; for text, recalled tokens re-use their stored positional indices to preserve syntax/section cues. The local branch handles most computation, while the global branch is reserved for the small recalled set, keeping total attention cost bounded.

Memory maintenance combines consolidation, deduplication, and eviction. We update existing memory entries with an uncertainty-weighted exponential moving average so novel or ambiguous content is written more strongly, while routine content produces smaller updates. To prevent bloat, we filter redundancies by semantic similarity with a recency-aware policy and evict via a relevance–age score. The selector and gates are trained end-to-end with auxiliary supervision that encourages retrieved memory to overlap with gold evidence when available; hard retrieval is approximated with differentiable top-k or Gumbel sampling during training and replaced by ANN search over viewer keys at serving time.

Across modalities, SQCM drops the need for full O(T^2) attention over long contexts: processing is O(T) for local compute, plus O(log B) for ANN over a bounded memory B and O(M·K) attention on recalled tokens (M segments, K tokens each). Typical settings (e.g., k=8 viewer keys per segment, M≤4, K≤64) provide robust recall of distant evidence while preserving a small, predictable compute budget.

### Rationale
Long-context models struggle because they (a) pay quadratic cost for indiscriminate attention and (b) diffuse attention mass over many irrelevant tokens, causing forgetting of distant but crucial evidence. SQCM attacks both by compressing history into query-addressable, multi-view keys and a tiny set of core tokens, and by retrieving only what the current state deems relevant. Multi-view keys preserve diverse semantics so a single segment can be recalled for many future questions without dense late interaction, while core tokens plus positional indices retain the fine-grained structure needed to reason once retrieved.

Streaming design and alignment maintain fidelity over time: in video, warping reduces temporal drift; in text/episodes, stored indices anchor recalled tokens to their original structure. Uncertainty-weighted consolidation prevents catastrophic overwriting while redundancy filtering keeps memory lean. The dual-branch encoder further reduces compute by reserving expensive global attention for a handful of recalled tokens, letting a cheap local pathway handle most steps. Together, these choices yield effective, scalable memory of distant information under tight compute budgets.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

2. [MULTIMODAL] Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers (2410.05800)
   problem: In sequential/continual training, replay buffers typically store whole instances (coresets), which is memory-inefficient for transformer models where only a subset of tokens per sample is truly informative. This leads to unnecessary storage and weaker performance-memory tradeoffs.
   method:  Introduce core tokensets: a two-stage, token-level data summarization for transformers. First, select a subset of important samples using coreset methods (e.g., GradMatch/CRAIG). Then, within those samples, select only the most influential tokens via attention-based attribution maps (GradLRP combining gradients with layer-wise relevance propagation, Atman perturbing pre-softmax attention guided by token similarity, Grad-CAM, Rollout). Store token values and their positional indices to preserve structure during replay. Across image classification, open-ended VQA, and image captioning, core tokensets consistently outperform traditional coresets at the same memory and can match or surpass much larger coresets (e.g., ~1% core tokenset comparable to ≥2–10% coreset).

3. [CV] ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers (2405.04299)
   problem: Temporal modeling for multi-camera occupancy often relies on single-frame interaction or sliding-window training but online inference, causing train-test mismatch and extra computation; directly interacting all voxel queries with multi-frame histories is expensive and noisy due to many empty voxels.
   method:  Propose streaming temporal attention using an online BEV-level FIFO memory of multi-frame features with ego-motion compensation via feature warping. Voxel queries are squeezed into BEV queries for deformable-attention interaction with aligned memory frames and then unsqueezed back to voxels, reducing compute and denoising by operating on BEV cells. The BEV-to-BEV design improves occupancy and flow quality over voxel-to-BEV (≈+0.9 mIoU and notably lower mAVE) with negligible FPS overhead, and longer memory (N>1) further boosts performance. Consistent online streaming training also cuts training time versus sliding-window (≈36h→13h) while improving accuracy.

This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
