messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?


### Proposed method
We propose Streaming Query-Conditioned Compressive Memory (SQCM), a modular architecture that separates short-range computation from long-range recall. A lightweight dual-branch encoder processes the incoming stream (text tokens, video frames, or state-action tuples) with cheap local modeling and a tightly windowed global self-attention, while a long-term memory bank is built online as a sparse, query-addressable store. Each stream segment (e.g., 512–1k text tokens, a short video clip, or N steps of an episode) is summarized into a small set of multi-view keys and a tiny cache of core tokens; future steps retrieve only from this bank instead of attending over the full past, keeping compute sub-quadratic and near-constant with stream length.

For ingestion, each segment produces k multi-view keys by inserting special viewer tokens that learn to capture diverse semantics of the same content. In parallel, we compute token-level importance within the segment using attention/gradient attribution and select a few core tokens along with their positional indices. The memory thus stores a two-tier entry per segment: (1) multi-view keys for fast approximate addressing and (2) a compact value set of core tokens that preserves structure and can be re-inserted into attention when needed. For videos, we maintain a small FIFO of recent segment features and apply motion/ego-compensation to align them before summarization; for text/episodes we retain absolute or section-relative positions to enable structural alignment.

At inference time, the current model state (decoder state, task head, or explicit query vector) soft-matches against the memory’s viewer keys using a max-pooled similarity over views, retrieving the top-M relevant segments. Only the union of these segments’ core tokens (e.g., 2–4 segments × 16–64 tokens each) is brought into the context via cross-attention, gated by a learned relevance score so that the model pays for long-range computation only when evidence is likely downstream. For streaming video, recalled features are optionally warped into the current frame’s reference before cross-attention to reduce noise; for text, recalled tokens re-use their stored positional indices to preserve syntax/section cues. The local branch handles most computation, while the global branch is reserved for the small recalled set, keeping total attention cost bounded.

Memory maintenance combines consolidation, deduplication, and eviction. We update existing memory entries with an uncertainty-weighted exponential moving average so novel or ambiguous content is written more strongly, while routine content produces smaller updates. To prevent bloat, we filter redundancies by semantic similarity with a recency-aware policy and evict via a relevance–age score. The selector and gates are trained end-to-end with auxiliary supervision that encourages retrieved memory to overlap with gold evidence when available; hard retrieval is approximated with differentiable top-k or Gumbel sampling during training and replaced by ANN search over viewer keys at serving time.

Across modalities, SQCM drops the need for full O(T^2) attention over long contexts: processing is O(T) for local compute, plus O(log B) for ANN over a bounded memory B and O(M·K) attention on recalled tokens (M segments, K tokens each). Typical settings (e.g., k=8 viewer keys per segment, M≤4, K≤64) provide robust recall of distant evidence while preserving a small, predictable compute budget.

### Rationale
Long-context models struggle because they (a) pay quadratic cost for indiscriminate attention and (b) diffuse attention mass over many irrelevant tokens, causing forgetting of distant but crucial evidence. SQCM attacks both by compressing history into query-addressable, multi-view keys and a tiny set of core tokens, and by retrieving only what the current state deems relevant. Multi-view keys preserve diverse semantics so a single segment can be recalled for many future questions without dense late interaction, while core tokens plus positional indices retain the fine-grained structure needed to reason once retrieved.

Streaming design and alignment maintain fidelity over time: in video, warping reduces temporal drift; in text/episodes, stored indices anchor recalled tokens to their original structure. Uncertainty-weighted consolidation prevents catastrophic overwriting while redundancy filtering keeps memory lean. The dual-branch encoder further reduces compute by reserving expensive global attention for a handful of recalled tokens, letting a cheap local pathway handle most steps. Together, these choices yield effective, scalable memory of distant information under tight compute budgets.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

2. [MULTIMODAL] Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers (2410.05800)
   problem: In sequential/continual training, replay buffers typically store whole instances (coresets), which is memory-inefficient for transformer models where only a subset of tokens per sample is truly informative. This leads to unnecessary storage and weaker performance-memory tradeoffs.
   method:  Introduce core tokensets: a two-stage, token-level data summarization for transformers. First, select a subset of important samples using coreset methods (e.g., GradMatch/CRAIG). Then, within those samples, select only the most influential tokens via attention-based attribution maps (GradLRP combining gradients with layer-wise relevance propagation, Atman perturbing pre-softmax attention guided by token similarity, Grad-CAM, Rollout). Store token values and their positional indices to preserve structure during replay. Across image classification, open-ended VQA, and image captioning, core tokensets consistently outperform traditional coresets at the same memory and can match or surpass much larger coresets (e.g., ~1% core tokenset comparable to ≥2–10% coreset).

3. [CV] ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers (2405.04299)
   problem: Temporal modeling for multi-camera occupancy often relies on single-frame interaction or sliding-window training but online inference, causing train-test mismatch and extra computation; directly interacting all voxel queries with multi-frame histories is expensive and noisy due to many empty voxels.
   method:  Propose streaming temporal attention using an online BEV-level FIFO memory of multi-frame features with ego-motion compensation via feature warping. Voxel queries are squeezed into BEV queries for deformable-attention interaction with aligned memory frames and then unsqueezed back to voxels, reducing compute and denoising by operating on BEV cells. The BEV-to-BEV design improves occupancy and flow quality over voxel-to-BEV (≈+0.9 mIoU and notably lower mAVE) with negligible FPS overhead, and longer memory (N>1) further boosts performance. Consistent online streaming training also cuts training time versus sliding-window (≈36h→13h) while improving accuracy.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Video QA models lack large-scale, fine-grained datasets that preserve essential spatial details, temporal coherence, and intermediate reasoning steps. Existing automatic pipelines either rely on coarse metadata or exhaustively parse redundant frames, limiting scalability and fine-grained reasoning.
   method:  Introduce VideoEspresso, an automated pipeline that builds 203K+ VideoQA pairs with multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT). It first maps sampled frames to language with InternVL2-8B captions and removes redundancy via BGE-M3 semantic similarity with a LIFO filter, then groups consecutive captions and prompts GPT-4o to generate QA pairs with an auxiliary LLM for quality filtering. For CoT, GPT-4o selects core-frame captions, extracts key objects, and writes textual evidence; GroundingDINO with CLIP verification adds spatial bounding boxes, and BGE-M3 retrieves temporal grounding to align CoT with original frames.

7. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

8. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
   method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

9. [ROBOTICS] UniAff: A Unified Representation of Affordances for Tool Usage and Articulation with Vision-Language Models (2409.20551)
   problem: Vision-language models struggle to jointly capture fine-grained local affordances and global 3D motion constraints from images, especially under resolution limits and with single-encoder designs.
   method:  UniAff fine-tunes an MLLM based on SPHINX with an LLaMA2 backbone using an 'Any Resolution' strategy that tiles 448×448 inputs into sub-images to preserve details. A mixed visual encoder fuses CLIP (local semantics), DINOv2 (enhanced local detail), and Q-Former (global summary), concatenating features along channels for robust grounding. Training casts ψ prediction as VQA with cross-entropy, jointly fine-tuning visual projection layers and the language model; decoded manipulation parameters are produced via a De-Tokenizer. This design yields strong generalization, improving over LISA on HANDAL and surpassing A3VLM on unseen articulated manipulation settings.

10. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

12. [MULTIMODAL] BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps (2005.04625)
   problem: Direct reinforcement learning on long-horizon VLN instructions is unstable, and imitation learning on whole trajectories does not adequately prepare agents for long, uncertain executions.
   method:  Use a two-phase training pipeline: (1) micro-level imitation learning where each BABY-STEP is learned independently with the agent 'preset' in expert context to match expert sub-trajectories; (2) curriculum-based reinforcement learning that progressively increases the number of consecutive BABY-STEPs the agent must execute (lectures), providing expert history up to the start and optimizing a fidelity-oriented reward (CLS) via policy gradients. This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.

13. [IR_REC] Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval (2203.08372)
   problem: Bi-encoder dense retrievers compress each document into a single vector, which struggles to match documents that can answer multiple, diverse queries; multi-vector alternatives often require dense interactions that break ANN-friendly first-stage retrieval.
   method:  Introduce multiple [Viewer] tokens into the document input to produce k document embeddings that capture different semantic views, replacing the single [CLS]-based representation. The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

14. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)
   problem: Inference-time relevance feedback for retrievers typically relies on cross-encoder scores and KL divergence, but listwise LLM rerankers output only orderings, making standard distillation inapplicable and potentially missing stronger supervision signals.

1. CacheFlow: Compressive Streaming Memory for Efficient Long-Form Video Understanding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Long-form video question answering (VQA) overwhelms current vision-language models (VLMs) because attention and key-value (KV) caches grow with runtime, forcing either expensive inference or near-sighted sliding windows. We introduce CacheFlow, a training-free pipeline that pairs Dynamic Token Dropping (DTD) with a compressive long-term memory. DTD prunes per-patch tokens online via cosine similarity to the previous frame, and surviving tokens are packed into fixed-size blocks. This online, per-frame processing makes our approach fundamentally suited for live streaming VQA. As blocks are processed, each one's keys are summarized by a tiny recurrent encoder to form a retrieval index, while the block's full KV pairs are offloaded and later rehydrated for generation, preserving answer fidelity. At inference, a consensus-based retrieval mechanism retrieves only the Top-K most relevant blocks and attends over both the retrieved and local context for precise, long-range reasoning. CacheFlow is drop-in, architecture-agnostic, and requires no fine-tuning. Experiments on both offline and streaming VQA benchmarks demonstrate that CacheFlow outperforms current strong baselines, while processing up to 87% less tokens. Our dual approach enables VLMs to be both efficient and context-aware, paving the way for practical long-form video understanding.

Under a unified LongBench evaluation protocol with fixed prompting, decoding, and matched token budgets, S3-Hybrid closely matches full-context inference across multiple model families and improves robustness in several information-dense settings. This paper introduces TokenSkipping, a practical and efficient KV cache pruning method designed to mitigate these challenges. TokenSkipping is a training-free, stateless, pre-storage method that employs a lightweight heuristic based on the L2 norm of key vectors, combined with a novel quota-based streaming algorithm for auto-regressive decoding. By deciding whether to admit or skip each token before writing to the cache, it eliminates cache-wide re-scoring and remains compatible with fused-attention kernels and Grouped Query Attention. A dual-mode design matches practical inference: during prefill, the policy retains the top-k highest-norm tokens under a fixed budget; during decoding, a streaming rule compares each new token’s norm to a reservoir-sampled percentile threshold, guaranteeing strict memory bounds with O(1) overhead. Anchor tokens are preserved to stabilize attention, and the policy operates independently per layer without retraining or kernel changes. Evaluated on Llama 3.1 8B (tested up to 128k tokens) and Mixtral-8 × 7B within their supported context windows, TokenSkipping substantially reduces KV cache memory while keeping throughput competitive with unpruned inference and cache-wide norm-based baselines. Our results reveal a distinct trade-off profile: aggressive pruning degrades fine-grained retrieval tasks (such as Needle-In-A-Haystack and QA), but TokenSkipping maintains robust performance on semantic summarization and coherence benchmarks, indicating that norm-based pruning can preserve global semantic context even when local precision is compressed. Compared to naive baselines like Sliding Window, TokenSkipping achieves better coherence under similar memory budgets and avoids global scoring, offering a simple, high-throughput optimization for memory-constrained long-context deployments.

5. ILRe: Intermediate Layer Retrieval for Context Compression in Causal Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated success across many benchmarks. Implemented as a 14B-parameter model and trained on a curated Unreal Engine-rendered dataset, RELIC achieves real-time generation at 16 FPS while demonstrating more accurate action following, more stable long-horizon streaming, and more robust spatial-memory retrieval compared with prior work. 1. Near-Oracle KV Selection via Pre-hoc Sparsity for Long-Context Inference (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   A core bottleneck in large language model (LLM) inference is the cost of attending over the ever-growing key-value (KV) cache. Although near-oracle top-k KV selection can preserve the quality of dense attention while sharply reducing computation and bandwidth, existing sparse methods generally rely on posterior heuristics, i.e., selectors conditioned on observed attention or proxy scores. Such conditioning introduces posterior bias: it tends to distort true token importance and miss salient tokens, thereby impairing long-range reasoning. To tackle this problem, we propose Pre-hoc Sparsity (PrHS), which selects KV entries before attention scoring and provides explicit accuracy control. Let the attention mass of discarded entries be delta (the dropped mass). Through a marginal-to-mutual-information analysis, we derive an upper bound on the mutual-information loss that depends only on the dropped mass. This relation explains failure modes of posterior heuristics and enables verifiable guarantees by controlling the dropped mass in advance. Within PrHS, we instantiate three orthogonal pre-hoc selectors along the axes of time, depth, and layer. Extensive experiments on LLaMA and Mistral families validate PrHS. Across GSM8K and CoQA, PrHS reduces retrieval overhead by over 90%, achieving 3x higher retrieval sparsity than HShare at matched or better accuracy. It incurs under 1% average degradation on LongBench, lowers attention FLOPs by about 15% versus prior sparse baselines, and yields a 9.9x speedup in attention-operator latency and 2.8x higher throughput on NVIDIA A100-80GB GPUs than the dense baseline.

HSA divides inputs into chunks, selects the top-$k$ chunks and hierarchically aggregates information. The core innovation lies in learning token-to-chunk relevance based on fine-grained token-level information inside each chunk. This approach enhances the precision of chunk selection across both in-domain and out-of-domain context lengths. To make HSA efficient, we further introduce a hardware-aligned kernel design. By combining HSA with Mamba, we introduce RAMba, which achieves perfect accuracy in passkey retrieval across 64 million contexts despite pre-training on only 4K-length contexts, and significant improvements on various downstream tasks, with nearly constant memory footprint. These results show RAMba's huge potential in long-context modeling.

3. Cache What Lasts: Token Retention for Memory-Bounded KV Cache in LLMs (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 5)
   Memory and computation remain core bottlenecks in long-horizon LLM inference due to the quadratic cost of self-attention and the ever-growing key-value (KV) cache. Existing strategies for memory-bounded inference, such as quantization, offloading, or heuristic KV eviction, either incur high orchestration costs or rely on unreliable attention-based proxies of importance. We propose TRIM-KV, a novel approach that learns each token's intrinsic importance at creation time via a lightweight retention gate. Each gate predicts a scalar retention score that decays over time, reflecting the long-term utility of the token for a specific layer and head. Tokens with low scores are evicted when the memory budget is exceeded, ensuring that the cache always contains the most critical tokens. TRIM-KV is trained efficiently through distillation from a frozen LLM combined with a capacity loss, requiring only gate fine-tuning and adding negligible inference overhead. Across mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME24), procedural generation (LongProc), conversational long-memory benchmarks (LongMemEval), and long-context understanding (LongBenchV2 and SCBench), TRIM-KV consistently outperforms strong eviction and learnable retrieval baselines, especially in low-memory regimes. Remarkably, it even surpasses full-cache models in some settings, showing that selective retention can serve as a form of regularization, suppressing noise from uninformative tokens. Qualitative analyses further reveal that learned retention scores align with human intuition, naturally recovering heuristics such as sink tokens, sliding windows, and gist compression without explicit design. Beyond efficiency, retention scores provide insights into layer- and head-specific roles, suggesting a new path toward LLM interpretability.

4. Understanding and Improving Length Generalization in Hierarchical Sparse Attention Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Effectively processing long contexts is a critical challenge for language models. While standard Transformers are limited by quadratic complexity and poor length extrapolation, alternative architectures like sliding window attention and state space models sacrifice the ability to effectively utilize the full context due to their fixed-size memory. Chunk-based sparse attention has emerged as a promising paradigm for extreme length generalization, yet the key architectural principles underpinning its success are not yet fully understood. In this work, we present a systematic dissection of these models to identify the core components driving their performance. Through a unified framework and comprehensive ablation studies, we demonstrate that a combination of three design principles is critical: (1) an expressive, non-linear Chunk Encoder with a dedicated CLS token to produce representations for retrieval; (2) a Bypassing Residual Path to stably integrate retrieved global information without it being overridden by the local residual stream; and (3) enforced selection sparsity during pre-training to bridge the train-test distribution gap. We provide a theoretical motivation for intra-chunk information processing and landmark generation. By combining these principles, we establish a new state-of-the-art for training-free length extrapolation, successfully generalizing models trained on a 4K context to 32 million tokens on RULER and BABILong. Our findings provide a clear and empirically-grounded set of design principles for developing future, highly-capable long-context language models.

5. EMS: Adaptive Evict-then-Merge Strategy for Head-wise KV Cache Compression Based on Global-Local Importance (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 3)
   As large language models (LLMs) continue to advance, the demand for higher quality and faster processing of long contexts across various applications is growing. KV cache is widely adopted as it stores previously generated key and value tokens, effectively reducing redundant computations during inference. However, as memory overhead becomes a significant concern, efficient compression of KV cache has gained increasing attention. Most existing methods perform compression from two perspectives: identifying important tokens and designing compression strategies. However, these approaches often produce biased distributions of important tokens due to the influence of accumulated attention scores or positional encoding. Furthermore, they overlook the sparsity and redundancy across different heads, which leads to difficulties in preserving the most effective information at the head level. To this end, we propose EMS to overcome these limitations, while achieving better KV cache compression under extreme compression ratios. Specifically, we introduce a Global-Local score that combines accumulated attention scores from both global and local KV tokens to better identify the token importance. For the compression strategy, we design an adaptive and unified Evict-then-Merge framework that accounts for the sparsity and redundancy of KV tokens across different heads. Additionally, we implement the head-wise parallel compression through a zero-class mechanism to enhance efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate our SOTA performance even under extreme compression ratios. EMS consistently achieves the lowest perplexity, improves scores by over 1.28 points across four LLMs on LongBench under a 256 cache budget, and preserves 95% retrieval accuracy with a cache budget less than 2% of the context length in the Needle-in-a-Haystack task.

6. Learn from the Past: Fast Sparse Indexing for Large Language Model Decoding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   As large language models (LLMs) continue to support increasingly longer contexts, the memory demand for key-value (KV) caches during decoding grows rapidly, becoming a critical bottleneck in both GPU memory capacity and PCIe bandwidth. Sparse attention mechanisms alleviate this issue by computing attention weights only for selected key-value pairs. However, their indexing computation typically requires traversing all key vectors, resulting in significant computational and data transfer overhead. To reduce the cost of index retrieval, existing methods often treat each decoding step as an independent process, failing to exploit the temporal correlations embedded in historical decoding information. To this end, we propose LFPS(Learn From the Past for Sparse Indexing), an acceleration method that dynamically constructs sparse indexing candidates based on historical attention patterns. LFPS captures two prevalent trends in decoder attention -vertical patterns (attending to fixed positions) and slash patterns (attending to relative positions) -and incorporates a positional expansion strategy to effectively predict the Top-k indices for the current step. We validate LFPS on challenging long-context benchmarks such as LongBench-RULER, using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as the base model. Experimental results show that LFPS achieves up to 22.8$\times$ speedup over full attention and 9.6$\times$ speedup over exact Top-k retrieval on an RTX 4090 GPU and a single CPU core of a Xeon Gold 6430, respectively, while preserving generation accuracy. These results demonstrate that LFPS offers a practical and efficient solution for decoding optimization in long-context LLM inference.

7. Improving Knowledge Management by means of Lean Thinking: A case study of project Lessons Learned exchange at the Engineering department of Janssen Biologics ( 2015, cites: 0)

1. A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW OF DEMAND FORECASTING MODELS FOR RETAIL E-COMMERCE ENHANCING ACCURACY IN INVENTORY AND DELIVERY PLANNING (International Journal of Scientific Interdisciplinary Research 2025, cites: 16)
   In the rapidly evolving landscape of retail e-commerce, characterized by volatile consumer behavior, diverse product assortments, and fluctuating market conditions, demand forecasting has emerged as a critical strategic capability. Accurate demand forecasting not only underpins effective inventory management and delivery planning but also serves as a cornerstone for optimizing supply chain responsiveness, minimizing stockouts and overstock situations, and enhancing customer satisfaction. As e-commerce platforms increasingly embrace data-driven operations, the role of predictive analytics in shaping demand planning strategies has gained unprecedented prominence. This systematic review presents a comprehensive synthesis of 72 peer-reviewed academic articles, industry reports, and empirical case studies published between 2010 and 2024. The objective is to evaluate the breadth, depth, and evolution of demand forecasting methodologies specifically applied within the context of retail e-commerce. The selected studies were rigorously screened and categorized based on methodological foundations, technological sophistication, and practical applications. Four major categories of forecasting models are examined: (1) traditional statistical approaches, such as AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA), Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA), and exponential smoothing models; (2) machine learning techniques, including decision trees, random forests, support vector regression, and ensemble methods; (3) hybrid frameworks that integrate statistical modeling with machine learning or deep learning components; and (4) advanced deep learning architectures, particularly Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, Gated Recurrent Units (GRUs), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and transformer-based models. The findings reveal distinct performance advantages across model categories. Traditional statistical models demonstrate continued relevance in scenarios marked by stable demand patterns, short forecasting horizons, and limited data complexity. However, their limitations become evident when applied to highly nonlinear, sparse, or volatile datasets. In contrast, machine learning models offer enhanced adaptability and accuracy, especially in handling high-dimensional data environments with diverse product lines and unpredictable promotional impacts. Deep learning models further advance this capability by capturing complex temporal dynamics, long-range dependencies, and multivariate input streams. These models are particularly effective for SKU-level forecasting, where product-specific demand patterns fluctuate frequently and require real-time recalibration. Many reviewed studies adopted modular architectures that allow domain-specific tuning and facilitate deployment within enterprise resource planning (ERP) and inventory management systems.

2. Adaptive fuzzy modeling of interval-valued stream data and application in cryptocurrencies prediction (Neural computing & applications (Print) 2021, cites: 15)
   <no abstract>

3. Probabilistic computation underlying sequence learning in a spiking attractor memory network (BMC Neuroscience 2013, cites: 2)
   Many cognitive and motor functions are enabled by the temporal representation and processing of stimuli but it remains an open issue how neuronal circuits could reliably encode such sequences of information. We consider the task of generating and learning spatiotemporal spike patterns in the context of an attractor memory network, in which each memory is stored in a distributed fashion represented by increased firing in pools of excitatory neurons. Excitatory activity is locally modulated by inhibitory neurons representing lateral inhibition that generates a type of winner-take-all dynamics. Networks of this type have previously been shown to exhibit switching between a non-coding ground state and low-rate memory state activations displaying gamma oscillations [1]; however, stable sequential associations between different attractors were not present. 
 
Assuming a probabilistic framework in which local neuron populations discretely encode uncertainty about an attribute in the external world (e.g. a column in visual cortex tuned to a specific edge orientation), we model inter-module synapses using the Bayesian Confidence Propagation Neural Network (BCPNN) plasticity rule [2]. We use a spike-based version of BCPNN in which synaptic weights are statistically inferred by estimating the posterior likelihood of activation for the postsynaptic cell upon presentation of evidence in the form of presynaptic activity patterns. Probabilities are estimated on-line using local exponentially weighted moving averages, with time scales that are biologically motivated by the cascade of events involved in the induction and maintenance of long-term plasticity. Modulating the kinetics of these traces is shown to shape the width of the STDP kernel, which in turn allows attractors to be learned forwards or backwards through time. Stable learning is confirmed by aunimodal stationary weight distribution. Inference additionally requires modification of a distinct neuronal component, which we interpret as a correlate of intrinsic excitability. Such synaptic [3] and nonsynaptic [4] mechanisms were specifically shown to be relevant for learning and inference. In broader terms, our model instead suggests the presence of and interaction between all of these processes in approximating Bayesian computation. 
 
Introducing plastic BCPNN synaptic projections into the attractor network model allows for stable associations between distinct network states. Associations are mediated by different synaptic timescales [5] with fast (AMPA type) and slower (NMDA type) dynamics that in conjunction with the spiking BCPNN rule produce sequences of attractor activations. We demonstrate the feasibility of our model using network simulations of integrate-and-fire neurons, and find that the ability to learn sequences depends on the specific structure of the inhibitory microcircuitry and on the local balance of excitation and inhibition in the network. Preliminary results show that the network can reliably store spatiotemporal patterns consisting of hundreds of discrete network states using just a few thousand neurons. Moreover, excitatory pools can participate multiple times in the sequence, suggesting that spiking attractor networks of this type could support an efficient combinatorial code. Our model provides novel insights into how local and global computations found throughout neocortex and hippocampus, framed in the context of probabilistic inference, could contribute to generating and learning sequential neural activity.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?


### Proposed method
We propose Streaming Query-Conditioned Compressive Memory (SQCM), a modular architecture that separates short-range computation from long-range recall. A lightweight dual-branch encoder processes the incoming stream (text tokens, video frames, or state-action tuples) with cheap local modeling and a tightly windowed global self-attention, while a long-term memory bank is built online as a sparse, query-addressable store. Each stream segment (e.g., 512–1k text tokens, a short video clip, or N steps of an episode) is summarized into a small set of multi-view keys and a tiny cache of core tokens; future steps retrieve only from this bank instead of attending over the full past, keeping compute sub-quadratic and near-constant with stream length.

For ingestion, each segment produces k multi-view keys by inserting special viewer tokens that learn to capture diverse semantics of the same content. In parallel, we compute token-level importance within the segment using attention/gradient attribution and select a few core tokens along with their positional indices. The memory thus stores a two-tier entry per segment: (1) multi-view keys for fast approximate addressing and (2) a compact value set of core tokens that preserves structure and can be re-inserted into attention when needed. For videos, we maintain a small FIFO of recent segment features and apply motion/ego-compensation to align them before summarization; for text/episodes we retain absolute or section-relative positions to enable structural alignment.

At inference time, the current model state (decoder state, task head, or explicit query vector) soft-matches against the memory’s viewer keys using a max-pooled similarity over views, retrieving the top-M relevant segments. Only the union of these segments’ core tokens (e.g., 2–4 segments × 16–64 tokens each) is brought into the context via cross-attention, gated by a learned relevance score so that the model pays for long-range computation only when evidence is likely downstream. For streaming video, recalled features are optionally warped into the current frame’s reference before cross-attention to reduce noise; for text, recalled tokens re-use their stored positional indices to preserve syntax/section cues. The local branch handles most computation, while the global branch is reserved for the small recalled set, keeping total attention cost bounded.

Memory maintenance combines consolidation, deduplication, and eviction. We update existing memory entries with an uncertainty-weighted exponential moving average so novel or ambiguous content is written more strongly, while routine content produces smaller updates. To prevent bloat, we filter redundancies by semantic similarity with a recency-aware policy and evict via a relevance–age score. The selector and gates are trained end-to-end with auxiliary supervision that encourages retrieved memory to overlap with gold evidence when available; hard retrieval is approximated with differentiable top-k or Gumbel sampling during training and replaced by ANN search over viewer keys at serving time.

Across modalities, SQCM drops the need for full O(T^2) attention over long contexts: processing is O(T) for local compute, plus O(log B) for ANN over a bounded memory B and O(M·K) attention on recalled tokens (M segments, K tokens each). Typical settings (e.g., k=8 viewer keys per segment, M≤4, K≤64) provide robust recall of distant evidence while preserving a small, predictable compute budget.

### Rationale
Long-context models struggle because they (a) pay quadratic cost for indiscriminate attention and (b) diffuse attention mass over many irrelevant tokens, causing forgetting of distant but crucial evidence. SQCM attacks both by compressing history into query-addressable, multi-view keys and a tiny set of core tokens, and by retrieving only what the current state deems relevant. Multi-view keys preserve diverse semantics so a single segment can be recalled for many future questions without dense late interaction, while core tokens plus positional indices retain the fine-grained structure needed to reason once retrieved.

Streaming design and alignment maintain fidelity over time: in video, warping reduces temporal drift; in text/episodes, stored indices anchor recalled tokens to their original structure. Uncertainty-weighted consolidation prevents catastrophic overwriting while redundancy filtering keeps memory lean. The dual-branch encoder further reduces compute by reserving expensive global attention for a handful of recalled tokens, letting a cheap local pathway handle most steps. Together, these choices yield effective, scalable memory of distant information under tight compute budgets.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

2. [MULTIMODAL] Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers (2410.05800)
   problem: In sequential/continual training, replay buffers typically store whole instances (coresets), which is memory-inefficient for transformer models where only a subset of tokens per sample is truly informative. This leads to unnecessary storage and weaker performance-memory tradeoffs.
   method:  Introduce core tokensets: a two-stage, token-level data summarization for transformers. First, select a subset of important samples using coreset methods (e.g., GradMatch/CRAIG). Then, within those samples, select only the most influential tokens via attention-based attribution maps (GradLRP combining gradients with layer-wise relevance propagation, Atman perturbing pre-softmax attention guided by token similarity, Grad-CAM, Rollout). Store token values and their positional indices to preserve structure during replay. Across image classification, open-ended VQA, and image captioning, core tokensets consistently outperform traditional coresets at the same memory and can match or surpass much larger coresets (e.g., ~1% core tokenset comparable to ≥2–10% coreset).

3. [CV] ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers (2405.04299)
   problem: Temporal modeling for multi-camera occupancy often relies on single-frame interaction or sliding-window training but online inference, causing train-test mismatch and extra computation; directly interacting all voxel queries with multi-frame histories is expensive and noisy due to many empty voxels.
   method:  Propose streaming temporal attention using an online BEV-level FIFO memory of multi-frame features with ego-motion compensation via feature warping. Voxel queries are squeezed into BEV queries for deformable-attention interaction with aligned memory frames and then unsqueezed back to voxels, reducing compute and denoising by operating on BEV cells. The BEV-to-BEV design improves occupancy and flow quality over voxel-to-BEV (≈+0.9 mIoU and notably lower mAVE) with negligible FPS overhead, and longer memory (N>1) further boosts performance. Consistent online streaming training also cuts training time versus sliding-window (≈36h→13h) while improving accuracy.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Video QA models lack large-scale, fine-grained datasets that preserve essential spatial details, temporal coherence, and intermediate reasoning steps. Existing automatic pipelines either rely on coarse metadata or exhaustively parse redundant frames, limiting scalability and fine-grained reasoning.
   method:  Introduce VideoEspresso, an automated pipeline that builds 203K+ VideoQA pairs with multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT). It first maps sampled frames to language with InternVL2-8B captions and removes redundancy via BGE-M3 semantic similarity with a LIFO filter, then groups consecutive captions and prompts GPT-4o to generate QA pairs with an auxiliary LLM for quality filtering. For CoT, GPT-4o selects core-frame captions, extracts key objects, and writes textual evidence; GroundingDINO with CLIP verification adds spatial bounding boxes, and BGE-M3 retrieves temporal grounding to align CoT with original frames.

7. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

8. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)
   problem: Foundation models tokenize images into large patches, missing the subtle, fine-grained local details that distinguish classes in specialized low-resource domains.
   method:  Without retraining the backbone, split the pre-trained ViT patch-embedding linear projection kernel K (e.g., q=14) into smaller sub-kernels (u=7, T=49) and apply them to subregions of each original patch to capture fine-grained local patterns. Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

9. [ROBOTICS] UniAff: A Unified Representation of Affordances for Tool Usage and Articulation with Vision-Language Models (2409.20551)
   problem: Vision-language models struggle to jointly capture fine-grained local affordances and global 3D motion constraints from images, especially under resolution limits and with single-encoder designs.
   method:  UniAff fine-tunes an MLLM based on SPHINX with an LLaMA2 backbone using an 'Any Resolution' strategy that tiles 448×448 inputs into sub-images to preserve details. A mixed visual encoder fuses CLIP (local semantics), DINOv2 (enhanced local detail), and Q-Former (global summary), concatenating features along channels for robust grounding. Training casts ψ prediction as VQA with cross-entropy, jointly fine-tuning visual projection layers and the language model; decoded manipulation parameters are produced via a De-Tokenizer. This design yields strong generalization, improving over LISA on HANDAL and surpassing A3VLM on unseen articulated manipulation settings.

10. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. 1. CacheFlow: Compressive Streaming Memory for Efficient Long-Form Video Understanding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Long-form video question answering (VQA) overwhelms current vision-language models (VLMs) because attention and key-value (KV) caches grow with runtime, forcing either expensive inference or near-sighted sliding windows. We introduce CacheFlow, a training-free pipeline that pairs Dynamic Token Dropping (DTD) with a compressive long-term memory. DTD prunes per-patch tokens online via cosine similarity to the previous frame, and surviving tokens are packed into fixed-size blocks. This online, per-frame processing makes our approach fundamentally suited for live streaming VQA. As blocks are processed, each one's keys are summarized by a tiny recurrent encoder to form a retrieval index, while the block's full KV pairs are offloaded and later rehydrated for generation, preserving answer fidelity. At inference, a consensus-based retrieval mechanism retrieves only the Top-K most relevant blocks and attends over both the retrieved and local context for precise, long-range reasoning. CacheFlow is drop-in, architecture-agnostic, and requires no fine-tuning. Experiments on both offline and streaming VQA benchmarks demonstrate that CacheFlow outperforms current strong baselines, while processing up to 87% less tokens. Our dual approach enables VLMs to be both efficient and context-aware, paving the way for practical long-form video understanding.

TokenSkipping is a training-free, stateless, pre-storage method that employs a lightweight heuristic based on the L2 norm of key vectors, combined with a novel quota-based streaming algorithm for auto-regressive decoding. By deciding whether to admit or skip each token before writing to the cache, it eliminates cache-wide re-scoring and remains compatible with fused-attention kernels and Grouped Query Attention. A dual-mode design matches practical inference: during prefill, the policy retains the top-k highest-norm tokens under a fixed budget; during decoding, a streaming rule compares each new token’s norm to a reservoir-sampled percentile threshold, guaranteeing strict memory bounds with O(1) overhead. Our results reveal a distinct trade-off profile: aggressive pruning degrades fine-grained retrieval tasks (such as Needle-In-A-Haystack and QA), but TokenSkipping maintains robust performance on semantic summarization and coherence benchmarks, indicating that norm-based pruning can preserve global semantic context even when local precision is compressed. Compared to naive baselines like Sliding Window, TokenSkipping achieves better coherence under similar memory budgets and avoids global scoring, offering a simple, high-throughput optimization for memory-constrained long-context deployments.

While many recent transformer models attempt to extend their context length over a million tokens, they remain impractical due to the quadratic time and space complexities. Although recent works on linear and sparse attention mechanisms can achieve this goal, their real-world applicability is often limited by the need to re-train from scratch and significantly worse performance. We consider the task of generating and learning spatiotemporal spike patterns in the context of an attractor memory network, in which each memory is stored in a distributed fashion represented by increased firing in pools of excitatory neurons. Excitatory activity is locally modulated by inhibitory neurons representing lateral inhibition that generates a type of winner-take-all dynamics. Networks of this type have previously been shown to exhibit switching between a non-coding ground state and low-rate memory state activations displaying gamma oscillations [1]; however, stable sequential associations between different attractors were not present. 
 
Assuming a probabilistic framework in which local neuron populations discretely encode uncertainty about an attribute in the external world (e.g. a column in visual cortex tuned to a specific edge orientation), we model inter-module synapses using the Bayesian Confidence Propagation Neural Network (BCPNN) plasticity rule [2]. Probabilities are estimated on-line using local exponentially weighted moving averages, with time scales that are biologically motivated by the cascade of events involved in the induction and maintenance of long-term plasticity. Modulating the kinetics of these traces is shown to shape the width of the STDP kernel, which in turn allows attractors to be learned forwards or backwards through time. Stable learning is confirmed by aunimodal stationary weight distribution. Inference additionally requires modification of a distinct neuronal component, which we interpret as a correlate of intrinsic excitability. Such synaptic [3] and nonsynaptic [4] mechanisms were specifically shown to be relevant for learning and inference. In broader terms, our model instead suggests the presence of and interaction between all of these processes in approximating Bayesian computation. 
 
Introducing plastic BCPNN synaptic projections into the attractor network model allows for stable associations between distinct network states. Associations are mediated by different synaptic timescales [5] with fast (AMPA type) and slower (NMDA type) dynamics that in conjunction with the spiking BCPNN rule produce sequences of attractor activations. We demonstrate the feasibility of our model using network simulations of integrate-and-fire neurons, and find that the ability to learn sequences depends on the specific structure of the inhibitory microcircuitry and on the local balance of excitation and inhibition in the network. Moreover, excitatory pools can participate multiple times in the sequence, suggesting that spiking attractor networks of this type could support an efficient combinatorial code. Our model provides novel insights into how local and global computations found throughout neocortex and hippocampus, framed in the context of probabilistic inference, could contribute to generating and learning sequential neural activity.
