messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?


### Proposed method
We propose Streaming Query-Conditioned Compressive Memory (SQCM), a modular architecture that separates short-range computation from long-range recall. A lightweight dual-branch encoder processes the incoming stream (text tokens, video frames, or state-action tuples) with cheap local modeling and a tightly windowed global self-attention, while a long-term memory bank is built online as a sparse, query-addressable store. Each stream segment (e.g., 512–1k text tokens, a short video clip, or N steps of an episode) is summarized into a small set of multi-view keys and a tiny cache of core tokens; future steps retrieve only from this bank instead of attending over the full past, keeping compute sub-quadratic and near-constant with stream length.

For ingestion, each segment produces k multi-view keys by inserting special viewer tokens that learn to capture diverse semantics of the same content. In parallel, we compute token-level importance within the segment using attention/gradient attribution and select a few core tokens along with their positional indices. The memory thus stores a two-tier entry per segment: (1) multi-view keys for fast approximate addressing and (2) a compact value set of core tokens that preserves structure and can be re-inserted into attention when needed. For videos, we maintain a small FIFO of recent segment features and apply motion/ego-compensation to align them before summarization; for text/episodes we retain absolute or section-relative positions to enable structural alignment.

At inference time, the current model state (decoder state, task head, or explicit query vector) soft-matches against the memory’s viewer keys using a max-pooled similarity over views, retrieving the top-M relevant segments. Only the union of these segments’ core tokens (e.g., 2–4 segments × 16–64 tokens each) is brought into the context via cross-attention, gated by a learned relevance score so that the model pays for long-range computation only when evidence is likely downstream. For streaming video, recalled features are optionally warped into the current frame’s reference before cross-attention to reduce noise; for text, recalled tokens re-use their stored positional indices to preserve syntax/section cues. The local branch handles most computation, while the global branch is reserved for the small recalled set, keeping total attention cost bounded.

Memory maintenance combines consolidation, deduplication, and eviction. We update existing memory entries with an uncertainty-weighted exponential moving average so novel or ambiguous content is written more strongly, while routine content produces smaller updates. To prevent bloat, we filter redundancies by semantic similarity with a recency-aware policy and evict via a relevance–age score. The selector and gates are trained end-to-end with auxiliary supervision that encourages retrieved memory to overlap with gold evidence when available; hard retrieval is approximated with differentiable top-k or Gumbel sampling during training and replaced by ANN search over viewer keys at serving time.

Across modalities, SQCM drops the need for full O(T^2) attention over long contexts: processing is O(T) for local compute, plus O(log B) for ANN over a bounded memory B and O(M·K) attention on recalled tokens (M segments, K tokens each). Typical settings (e.g., k=8 viewer keys per segment, M≤4, K≤64) provide robust recall of distant evidence while preserving a small, predictable compute budget.

### Rationale
Long-context models struggle because they (a) pay quadratic cost for indiscriminate attention and (b) diffuse attention mass over many irrelevant tokens, causing forgetting of distant but crucial evidence. SQCM attacks both by compressing history into query-addressable, multi-view keys and a tiny set of core tokens, and by retrieving only what the current state deems relevant. Multi-view keys preserve diverse semantics so a single segment can be recalled for many future questions without dense late interaction, while core tokens plus positional indices retain the fine-grained structure needed to reason once retrieved.

Streaming design and alignment maintain fidelity over time: in video, warping reduces temporal drift; in text/episodes, stored indices anchor recalled tokens to their original structure. Uncertainty-weighted consolidation prevents catastrophic overwriting while redundancy filtering keeps memory lean. The dual-branch encoder further reduces compute by reserving expensive global attention for a handful of recalled tokens, letting a cheap local pathway handle most steps. Together, these choices yield effective, scalable memory of distant information under tight compute budgets.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

2. [MULTIMODAL] Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers (2410.05800)
   problem: In sequential/continual training, replay buffers typically store whole instances (coresets), which is memory-inefficient for transformer models where only a subset of tokens per sample is truly informative. This leads to unnecessary storage and weaker performance-memory tradeoffs.
   method:  Introduce core tokensets: a two-stage, token-level data summarization for transformers. First, select a subset of important samples using coreset methods (e.g., GradMatch/CRAIG). Then, within those samples, select only the most influential tokens via attention-based attribution maps (GradLRP combining gradients with layer-wise relevance propagation, Atman perturbing pre-softmax attention guided by token similarity, Grad-CAM, Rollout). Store token values and their positional indices to preserve structure during replay. Across image classification, open-ended VQA, and image captioning, core tokensets consistently outperform traditional coresets at the same memory and can match or surpass much larger coresets (e.g., ~1% core tokenset comparable to ≥2–10% coreset).

3. [CV] ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers (2405.04299)
   problem: Temporal modeling for multi-camera occupancy often relies on single-frame interaction or sliding-window training but online inference, causing train-test mismatch and extra computation; directly interacting all voxel queries with multi-frame histories is expensive and noisy due to many empty voxels.
   method:  Propose streaming temporal attention using an online BEV-level FIFO memory of multi-frame features with ego-motion compensation via feature warping. Voxel queries are squeezed into BEV queries for deformable-attention interaction with aligned memory frames and then unsqueezed back to voxels, reducing compute and denoising by operating on BEV cells. The BEV-to-BEV design improves occupancy and flow quality over voxel-to-BEV (≈+0.9 mIoU and notably lower mAVE) with negligible FPS overhead, and longer memory (N>1) further boosts performance. Consistent online streaming training also cuts training time versus sliding-window (≈36h→13h) while improving accuracy.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)
   problem: Existing VQA systems lack explicit supervision that links questions/answers to the precise image regions that constitute visual evidence, leading to weak or misaligned attentions; related annotations like human attention maps are coarse and bounding boxes often do not directly answer diverse question types.
   method:  Introduce VQS, a dataset that explicitly links COCO instance segmentations to VQA question–answer pairs by asking annotators to select the segmentation(s) that visually answer each question (with tight boxes when needed). This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Video QA models lack large-scale, fine-grained datasets that preserve essential spatial details, temporal coherence, and intermediate reasoning steps. Existing automatic pipelines either rely on coarse metadata or exhaustively parse redundant frames, limiting scalability and fine-grained reasoning.
   method:  Introduce VideoEspresso, an automated pipeline that builds 203K+ VideoQA pairs with multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT). Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. 1. CacheFlow: Compressive Streaming Memory for Efficient Long-Form Video Understanding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Long-form video question answering (VQA) overwhelms current vision-language models (VLMs) because attention and key-value (KV) caches grow with runtime, forcing either expensive inference or near-sighted sliding windows. We introduce CacheFlow, a training-free pipeline that pairs Dynamic Token Dropping (DTD) with a compressive long-term memory. DTD prunes per-patch tokens online via cosine similarity to the previous frame, and surviving tokens are packed into fixed-size blocks. This online, per-frame processing makes our approach fundamentally suited for live streaming VQA. As blocks are processed, each one's keys are summarized by a tiny recurrent encoder to form a retrieval index, while the block's full KV pairs are offloaded and later rehydrated for generation, preserving answer fidelity. At inference, a consensus-based retrieval mechanism retrieves only the Top-K most relevant blocks and attends over both the retrieved and local context for precise, long-range reasoning. CacheFlow is drop-in, architecture-agnostic, and requires no fine-tuning. Experiments on both offline and streaming VQA benchmarks demonstrate that CacheFlow outperforms current strong baselines, while processing up to 87% less tokens. Our dual approach enables VLMs to be both efficient and context-aware, paving the way for practical long-form video understanding.

   Large language models are increasingly applied to multi-document and long-form inputs, yet long-context inference remains memory- and noise-inefficient. S3-Attention decodes transient key and query projections into top-k sparse feature identifiers using lightweight sparse autoencoders, and constructs a CPU-based inverted index mapping features to token positions or spans during a single streaming scan. This design allows the KV cache to be discarded entirely and bounds GPU memory usage by the scan chunk size. At generation time, feature co-activation is used to retrieve compact evidence spans, optionally fused with BM25 for exact lexical matching. Under a unified LongBench evaluation protocol with fixed prompting, decoding, and matched token budgets, S3-Hybrid closely matches full-context inference across multiple model families and improves robustness in several information-dense settings. We also report an engineering limitation of the current prototype, which incurs higher wall-clock latency than optimized full-KV baselines, motivating future kernel-level optimization.

Specifically, StreamMem encodes new video frames in a streaming manner, compressing the KV cache using attention scores between visual tokens and generic query tokens, while maintaining a fixed-size KV memory to enable efficient question answering (QA) in memory-constrained, long-video scenarios. Evaluation on three long video understanding and two streaming video question answering benchmarks shows that StreamMem achieves state-of-the-art performance in query-agnostic KV cache compression and is competitive with query-aware compression approaches.

   Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities, yet their deployment in long-context scenarios is severely hampered by the extensive memory and computational costs associated with the Key-Value (KV) cache. This paper introduces TokenSkipping, a practical and efficient KV cache pruning method designed to mitigate these challenges. TokenSkipping is a training-free, stateless, pre-storage method that employs a lightweight heuristic based on the L2 norm of key vectors, combined with a novel quota-based streaming algorithm for auto-regressive decoding. By deciding whether to admit or skip each token before writing to the cache, it eliminates cache-wide re-scoring and remains compatible with fused-attention kernels and Grouped Query Attention. A dual-mode design matches practical inference: during prefill, the policy retains the top-k highest-norm tokens under a fixed budget; during decoding, a streaming rule compares each new token’s norm to a reservoir-sampled percentile threshold, guaranteeing strict memory bounds with O(1) overhead. Anchor tokens are preserved to stabilize attention, and the policy operates independently per layer without retraining or kernel changes. Evaluated on Llama 3.1 8B (tested up to 128k tokens) and Mixtral-8 × 7B within their supported context windows, TokenSkipping substantially reduces KV cache memory while keeping throughput competitive with unpruned inference and cache-wide norm-based baselines. Our results reveal a distinct trade-off profile: aggressive pruning degrades fine-grained retrieval tasks (such as Needle-In-A-Haystack and QA), but TokenSkipping maintains robust performance on semantic summarization and coherence benchmarks, indicating that norm-based pruning can preserve global semantic context even when local precision is compressed. Compared to naive baselines like Sliding Window, TokenSkipping achieves better coherence under similar memory budgets and avoids global scoring, offering a simple, high-throughput optimization for memory-constrained long-context deployments.

5. ILRe: Intermediate Layer Retrieval for Context Compression in Causal Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated success across many benchmarks. However, they still exhibit limitations in long-context scenarios, primarily due to their short effective context length, quadratic computational complexity, and high memory overhead when processing lengthy inputs. To mitigate these issues, we introduce a novel context compression pipeline, called Intermediate Layer Retrieval (ILRe), which determines one intermediate decoder layer offline, encodes context by streaming chunked prefill only up to that layer, and recalls tokens by the attention scores between the input query and full key cache in that specified layer. In particular, we propose a multi-pooling kernels allocating strategy in the token recalling process to maintain the completeness of semantics. Our approach not only reduces the prefilling complexity from $O(L^2)$ to $O(L)$ and trims the memory footprint to a few tenths of that required for the full context, but also delivers performance comparable to or superior to the full-context setup in long-context scenarios. Without additional post training or operator development, ILRe can process a single $1M$ tokens request in less than half a minute (speedup $\approx 180\times$) and scores RULER-$1M$ benchmark of $\approx 79.8$ with model Llama-3.1-UltraLong-8B-1M-Instruct on a Huawei Ascend 910B NPU.

In this work, we present RELIC, a unified framework that tackles these three challenges altogether. Given a single image and a text description, RELIC enables memory-aware, long-duration exploration of arbitrary scenes in real time. Built upon recent autoregressive video-diffusion distillation techniques, our model represents long-horizon memory using highly compressed historical latent tokens encoded with both relative actions and absolute camera poses within the KV cache. This compact, camera-aware memory structure supports implicit 3D-consistent content retrieval and enforces long-term coherence with minimal computational overhead. In parallel, we fine-tune a bidirectional teacher video model to generate sequences beyond its original 5-second training horizon, and transform it into a causal student generator using a new memory-efficient self-forcing paradigm that enables full-context distillation over long-duration teacher as well as long student self-rollouts. Implemented as a 14B-parameter model and trained on a curated Unreal Engine-rendered dataset, RELIC achieves real-time generation at 16 FPS while demonstrating more accurate action following, more stable long-horizon streaming, and more robust spatial-memory retrieval compared with prior work. These capabilities establish RELIC as a strong foundation for the next generation of interactive world modeling.

However, the challenge of continuous and simultaneous streaming perception, memory, and reasoning remains largely unexplored. Current MLLMs are constrained by their sequence-to-sequence architecture, which limits their ability to process inputs and generate responses simultaneously, akin to being unable to think while perceiving. The proposed framework InternLM-XComposer2.5-OmniLive (IXC2.5-OL) consists of three key modules: (1) Streaming Perception Module: Processes multimodal information in real-time, storing key details in memory and triggering reasoning in response to user queries. (2) Multi-modal Long Memory Module: Integrates short-term and long-term memory, compressing short-term memories into long-term ones for efficient retrieval and improved accuracy. (3) Reasoning Module: Responds to queries and executes reasoning tasks, coordinating with the perception and memory modules. However, the current state-of-the-art (SOTA) models face significant challenges in event boundary detection, contextual understanding, and real-time processing, limiting their applicability to complex, multi-event videos. In this paper, we introduce CMSTR-ODE, a novel Cross-Modal Streaming Transformer with Neural ODE Temporal Localization framework for dense video captioning. Our model incorporates three key innovations: (1) Neural ODE-based Temporal Localization for continuous and efficient event boundary prediction, improving the accuracy of temporal segmentation; (2) cross-modal memory retrieval, which enriches video features with external textual knowledge, enabling more context-aware and descriptive captioning; and (3) a Streaming Multi-Scale Transformer Decoder that generates captions in real time, handling objects and events of varying scales. We evaluate CMSTR-ODE on two benchmark datasets, YouCook2, Flickr30k, and ActivityNet Captions, where it achieves SOTA performance, significantly outperforming existing models in terms of CIDEr, BLEU-4, and ROUGE scores. Our model also demonstrates superior computational efficiency, processing videos at 15 frames per second, making it suitable for real-time applications such as video surveillance and live video captioning. Ablation studies highlight the contributions of each component, confirming the effectiveness of our approach. By addressing the limitations of current methods, CMSTR-ODE sets a new benchmark for dense video captioning, offering a robust and scalable solution for both real-time and long-form video understanding tasks.

3. BitMar: Low-Bit Multimodal Fusion with Episodic Memory for Edge Devices (Proceedings of the First BabyLM Workshop 2025, cites: 1)
   Cross-attention transformers and other multimodal vision-language models excel at grounding and generation; however, their extensive, full-precision backbones make it challenging to deploy them on edge devices. Memory-augmented architectures enhance the utilization of past context; however, most works rarely pair them with aggressive edge-oriented quantization. We introduce BitMar, a quantized multimodal transformer that proposes an external human-like episodic memory for effective image-text generation on hardware with limited resources. BitMar utilizes 1.58-bit encoders, one for text (BitNet-style) and one for vision (DiNOv2-based), to create compact embeddings that are combined and used to query a fixed-size key-value episodic memory. During vector retrieval, the BitNet decoder applies per-layer conditioning, which increases the contextual relevance of generated content. The decoder also employs attention sinks with a sliding-window mechanism to process long or streaming inputs under tight memory budgets. The combination of per-layer conditioning and sliding-window attention achieves a strong quality-speed trade-off, delivering competitive captioning and multimodal understanding at low latency with a small model footprint. These characteristics make BitMar well-suited for edge deployment.

While many recent transformer models attempt to extend their context length over a million tokens, they remain impractical due to the quadratic time and space complexities. Although recent works on linear and sparse attention mechanisms can achieve this goal, their real-world applicability is often limited by the need to re-train from scratch and significantly worse performance. In response, we propose a novel approach, Hierarchically Pruned Attention (HiP), which reduces the time complexity of the attention mechanism to $O(T \log T)$ and the space complexity to $O(T)$, where $T$ is the sequence length. We notice a pattern in the attention scores of pretrained LLMs where tokens close together tend to have similar scores, which we call ``attention locality''. Based on this observation, we utilize a novel tree-search-like algorithm that estimates the top-$k$ key tokens for a given query on the fly, which is mathematically guaranteed to have better performance than random attention pruning. In addition to improving the time complexity of the attention mechanism, we further optimize GPU memory usage by implementing KV cache offloading, which stores only $O(\log T)$ tokens on the GPU while maintaining similar decoding throughput. Experiments on benchmarks show that HiP, with its training-free nature, significantly reduces both prefill and decoding latencies, as well as memory usage, while maintaining high-quality generation with minimal degradation. HiP enables pretrained LLMs to scale up to millions of tokens on commodity GPUs, potentially unlocking long-context LLM applications previously deemed infeasible.

Experimental results show that LFPS achieves up to 22.8$\times$ speedup over full attention and 9.6$\times$ speedup over exact Top-k retrieval on an RTX 4090 GPU and a single CPU core of a Xeon Gold 6430, respectively, while preserving generation accuracy. These results demonstrate that LFPS offers a practical and efficient solution for decoding optimization in long-context LLM inference.

   Many cognitive and motor functions are enabled by the temporal representation and processing of stimuli but it remains an open issue how neuronal circuits could reliably encode such sequences of information. We consider the task of generating and learning spatiotemporal spike patterns in the context of an attractor memory network, in which each memory is stored in a distributed fashion represented by increased firing in pools of excitatory neurons. Excitatory activity is locally modulated by inhibitory neurons representing lateral inhibition that generates a type of winner-take-all dynamics. Networks of this type have previously been shown to exhibit switching between a non-coding ground state and low-rate memory state activations displaying gamma oscillations [1]; however, stable sequential associations between different attractors were not present. 
 
Assuming a probabilistic framework in which local neuron populations discretely encode uncertainty about an attribute in the external world (e.g. a column in visual cortex tuned to a specific edge orientation), we model inter-module synapses using the Bayesian Confidence Propagation Neural Network (BCPNN) plasticity rule [2]. We use a spike-based version of BCPNN in which synaptic weights are statistically inferred by estimating the posterior likelihood of activation for the postsynaptic cell upon presentation of evidence in the form of presynaptic activity patterns. Probabilities are estimated on-line using local exponentially weighted moving averages, with time scales that are biologically motivated by the cascade of events involved in the induction and maintenance of long-term plasticity. Modulating the kinetics of these traces is shown to shape the width of the STDP kernel, which in turn allows attractors to be learned forwards or backwards through time. Stable learning is confirmed by aunimodal stationary weight distribution. Inference additionally requires modification of a distinct neuronal component, which we interpret as a correlate of intrinsic excitability. Such synaptic [3] and nonsynaptic [4] mechanisms were specifically shown to be relevant for learning and inference. In broader terms, our model instead suggests the presence of and interaction between all of these processes in approximating Bayesian computation. 
 
Introducing plastic BCPNN synaptic projections into the attractor network model allows for stable associations between distinct network states. Associations are mediated by different synaptic timescales [5] with fast (AMPA type) and slower (NMDA type) dynamics that in conjunction with the spiking BCPNN rule produce sequences of attractor activations. We demonstrate the feasibility of our model using network simulations of integrate-and-fire neurons, and find that the ability to learn sequences depends on the specific structure of the inhibitory microcircuitry and on the local balance of excitation and inhibition in the network. Preliminary results show that the network can reliably store spatiotemporal patterns consisting of hundreds of discrete network states using just a few thousand neurons. Moreover, excitatory pools can participate multiple times in the sequence, suggesting that spiking attractor networks of this type could support an efficient combinatorial code. Our model provides novel insights into how local and global computations found throughout neocortex and hippocampus, framed in the context of probabilistic inference, could contribute to generating and learning sequential neural activity.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Models attending to long contexts (videos, long documents,
episodes) suffer from quadratic attention cost and forgetting;
how can we maintain effective memory of distant information
without exhausting compute?


### Proposed method
We propose Streaming Query-Conditioned Compressive Memory (SQCM), a modular architecture that separates short-range computation from long-range recall. A lightweight dual-branch encoder processes the incoming stream (text tokens, video frames, or state-action tuples) with cheap local modeling and a tightly windowed global self-attention, while a long-term memory bank is built online as a sparse, query-addressable store. Each stream segment (e.g., 512–1k text tokens, a short video clip, or N steps of an episode) is summarized into a small set of multi-view keys and a tiny cache of core tokens; future steps retrieve only from this bank instead of attending over the full past, keeping compute sub-quadratic and near-constant with stream length.

For ingestion, each segment produces k multi-view keys by inserting special viewer tokens that learn to capture diverse semantics of the same content. In parallel, we compute token-level importance within the segment using attention/gradient attribution and select a few core tokens along with their positional indices. The memory thus stores a two-tier entry per segment: (1) multi-view keys for fast approximate addressing and (2) a compact value set of core tokens that preserves structure and can be re-inserted into attention when needed. For videos, we maintain a small FIFO of recent segment features and apply motion/ego-compensation to align them before summarization; for text/episodes we retain absolute or section-relative positions to enable structural alignment.

At inference time, the current model state (decoder state, task head, or explicit query vector) soft-matches against the memory’s viewer keys using a max-pooled similarity over views, retrieving the top-M relevant segments. Only the union of these segments’ core tokens (e.g., 2–4 segments × 16–64 tokens each) is brought into the context via cross-attention, gated by a learned relevance score so that the model pays for long-range computation only when evidence is likely downstream. For streaming video, recalled features are optionally warped into the current frame’s reference before cross-attention to reduce noise; for text, recalled tokens re-use their stored positional indices to preserve syntax/section cues. The local branch handles most computation, while the global branch is reserved for the small recalled set, keeping total attention cost bounded.

Memory maintenance combines consolidation, deduplication, and eviction. We update existing memory entries with an uncertainty-weighted exponential moving average so novel or ambiguous content is written more strongly, while routine content produces smaller updates. To prevent bloat, we filter redundancies by semantic similarity with a recency-aware policy and evict via a relevance–age score. The selector and gates are trained end-to-end with auxiliary supervision that encourages retrieved memory to overlap with gold evidence when available; hard retrieval is approximated with differentiable top-k or Gumbel sampling during training and replaced by ANN search over viewer keys at serving time.

Across modalities, SQCM drops the need for full O(T^2) attention over long contexts: processing is O(T) for local compute, plus O(log B) for ANN over a bounded memory B and O(M·K) attention on recalled tokens (M segments, K tokens each). Typical settings (e.g., k=8 viewer keys per segment, M≤4, K≤64) provide robust recall of distant evidence while preserving a small, predictable compute budget.

### Rationale
Long-context models struggle because they (a) pay quadratic cost for indiscriminate attention and (b) diffuse attention mass over many irrelevant tokens, causing forgetting of distant but crucial evidence. SQCM attacks both by compressing history into query-addressable, multi-view keys and a tiny set of core tokens, and by retrieving only what the current state deems relevant. Multi-view keys preserve diverse semantics so a single segment can be recalled for many future questions without dense late interaction, while core tokens plus positional indices retain the fine-grained structure needed to reason once retrieved.

Streaming design and alignment maintain fidelity over time: in video, warping reduces temporal drift; in text/episodes, stored indices anchor recalled tokens to their original structure. Uncertainty-weighted consolidation prevents catastrophic overwriting while redundancy filtering keeps memory lean. The dual-branch encoder further reduces compute by reserving expensive global attention for a handful of recalled tokens, letting a cheap local pathway handle most steps. Together, these choices yield effective, scalable memory of distant information under tight compute budgets.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [CV] Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world (2212.00972)
   problem: Transferring the generalization of a large cloud model to a lightweight device model under continual shifts while keeping downlink bandwidth low and mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

2. [MULTIMODAL] Core Tokensets for Data-efficient Sequential Training of Transformers (2410.05800)
   problem: In sequential/continual training, replay buffers typically store whole instances (coresets), which is memory-inefficient for transformer models where only a subset of tokens per sample is truly informative. This leads to unnecessary storage and weaker performance-memory tradeoffs.
   method:  Introduce core tokensets: a two-stage, token-level data summarization for transformers. First, select a subset of important samples using coreset methods (e.g., GradMatch/CRAIG). Then, within those samples, select only the most influential tokens via attention-based attribution maps (GradLRP combining gradients with layer-wise relevance propagation, Atman perturbing pre-softmax attention guided by token similarity, Grad-CAM, Rollout). Store token values and their positional indices to preserve structure during replay. Across image classification, open-ended VQA, and image captioning, core tokensets consistently outperform traditional coresets at the same memory and can match or surpass much larger coresets (e.g., ~1% core tokenset comparable to ≥2–10% coreset).

3. [CV] ViewFormer: Exploring Spatiotemporal Modeling for Multi-View 3D Occupancy Perception via View-Guided Transformers (2405.04299)
   problem: Temporal modeling for multi-camera occupancy often relies on single-frame interaction or sliding-window training but online inference, causing train-test mismatch and extra computation; directly interacting all voxel queries with multi-frame histories is expensive and noisy due to many empty voxels.
   method:  Propose streaming temporal attention using an online BEV-level FIFO memory of multi-frame features with ego-motion compensation via feature warping. Voxel queries are squeezed into BEV queries for deformable-attention interaction with aligned memory frames and then unsqueezed back to voxels, reducing compute and denoising by operating on BEV cells. The BEV-to-BEV design improves occupancy and flow quality over voxel-to-BEV (≈+0.9 mIoU and notably lower mAVE) with negligible FPS overhead, and longer memory (N>1) further boosts performance. Consistent online streaming training also cuts training time versus sliding-window (≈36h→13h) while improving accuracy.

4. [MULTIMODAL] VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (1708.04686)

This produces 37,868 images, 96,508 questions, 108,537 segmentations, and 43,725 boxes, enabling transfer of supervision between segmentation and VQA and supporting both supervised attention and new grounded tasks.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)

Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

6. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Video QA models lack large-scale, fine-grained datasets that preserve essential spatial details, temporal coherence, and intermediate reasoning steps. Existing automatic pipelines either rely on coarse metadata or exhaustively parse redundant frames, limiting scalability and fine-grained reasoning.
   method:  Introduce VideoEspresso, an automated pipeline that builds 203K+ VideoQA pairs with multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT). It first maps sampled frames to language with InternVL2-8B captions and removes redundancy via BGE-M3 semantic similarity with a LIFO filter, then groups consecutive captions and prompts GPT-4o to generate QA pairs with an auxiliary LLM for quality filtering. For CoT, GPT-4o selects core-frame captions, extracts key objects, and writes textual evidence; GroundingDINO with CLIP verification adds spatial bounding boxes, and BGE-M3 retrieves temporal grounding to align CoT with original frames.

7. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

8. [CV] Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models (2401.04716)

Learn a lightweight weighting vector to fuse sub-kernel feature maps into a patch-level feature, then max-pool and flatten so the token dimensionality aligns with the frozen foundation model. This parameter-efficient tokenization enhances attention to small local cues and outperforms state-of-the-art fine-grained methods under severe data scarcity.

9. [ROBOTICS] UniAff: A Unified Representation of Affordances for Tool Usage and Articulation with Vision-Language Models (2409.20551)

A mixed visual encoder fuses CLIP (local semantics), DINOv2 (enhanced local detail), and Q-Former (global summary), concatenating features along channels for robust grounding. Training casts ψ prediction as VQA with cross-entropy, jointly fine-tuning visual projection layers and the language model; decoded manipulation parameters are produced via a De-Tokenizer. This design yields strong generalization, improving over LISA on HANDAL and surpassing A3VLM on unseen articulated manipulation settings.

10. [ROBOTICS] Model Learning for Look-ahead Exploration in Continuous Control (1811.08086)
   problem: Learning long-horizon manipulation policies from sparse rewards is hard: action-level exploration (e.g., epsilon-greedy) is ineffective, while hierarchical option policies can restrict optimality.
   method:  HERLASE: first learn a small set of multi-goal skills (reach, grasp, transfer) with DDPG+HER. For each skill, learn (i) a coarse transition model T_i^coarse(s,g)→s_final and (ii) a success probability u_i(s,g) via neural regressors. During training on a new task, run a look-ahead tree search that samples skill identities and sub-goals, prunes using u_i (e.g., >0.5), predicts next states with T_i^coarse, and scores paths by the sum of skill critic values Q_i(s,g_i,a_i) plus a terminal distance-to-goal term. Execute the first skill’s policy π_i(s_t,g_i) until termination to collect exploratory experience, while the task policy is learned over low-level actions with DDPG+HER. Coarse skill dynamics enable long-horizon planning for exploration with reduced model-error accumulation.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

12. [MULTIMODAL] BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps (2005.04625)

This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.

The viewer tokens are randomly initialized and inserted before the document with position ids set to 0 to avoid shifting original token positions. At retrieval time, compute inner-products between the single query embedding and each document viewer embedding, and aggregate via max-pooling; index all viewer embeddings so the approach remains compatible with fast ANN search. This simple architectural change yields multi-view document representations without late-interaction overhead.

14. [IR_REC] FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2406.15657)

   method:  Use the LLM reranker’s ranked ordering as the supervision signal for updating retriever query embeddings, optimizing with the weighted RankNet loss over retrieved candidates to convert orderings into a differentiable objective. This enables relevance feedback without numeric scores and leverages the stronger listwise signal. On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

15. [IR_REC] Boosting Data Utilization for Multilingual Dense Retrieval (2509.09459)

This LLM-based filtering eliminates a substantial portion of false negatives (≈20% on average) and yields higher-quality negatives, outperforming strong TopK baselines (e.g., +2.5 nDCG@10 over TopK-PercPos on MIRACL).

Compared to a parallel DNC+ASR baseline, DNCASR yields lower speaker-attributed error (e.g., AMI-MDM Eval cpWER 31.5% with CDA vs 34.6% parallel; 9.0% relative reduction) and larger gains on multi-talker segments.

19. [ROBOTICS] Impedance Primitive-augmented Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Sequential Tasks (2508.19607)
   problem: Long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation requires adapting interaction stiffness, but existing hierarchical RL frameworks rely on static, position-only primitives, limiting robustness and safety during contact.
   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters. The predicted stiffness serves as an initial estimate for execution, enabling variable-impedance control within sequential primitives.    problem: Contact-rich manipulation is difficult to learn due to high-dimensional state/action spaces and uncertainty; prior methods hard-code how to exploit environmental constraints rather than letting robots autonomously create constraints that funnel uncertainty and speed up learning.

The outer loop observes a top-view depth image s_f0 and selects a fixture pose a_f, then receives as reward the inner loop’s cumulative return after a fixed number of episodes, incentivizing fixture placements that accelerate learning. It learns a CNN-based Q-function Q_w(s_f0, a_f) and uses the cross-entropy method at test time to select poses.    method:  A low-level controller performs closed-loop visual-force servoing toward the model’s visual-force goal, minimizing a joint objective over kinematic and force errors (with proportional gains and transformations) to issue incremental Cartesian end-effector and gripper motions, making the system insensitive to global calibration. Action primitives (e.g., approach, grasp, lift, pull, ungrasp) are appended to the text prompt and advanced via a simple state machine, enabling chaining of keyframes and error recovery. In real-robot ablations, removing forces reduces success to 50% over 20 trials and from 90% to 45% in paired trials; ablating depth, pre-training, augmentation, or text conditioning further degrades performance, evidencing the necessity of the joint visual-force objective and the overall system design.

1. CacheFlow: Compressive Streaming Memory for Efficient Long-Form Video Understanding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Long-form video question answering (VQA) overwhelms current vision-language models (VLMs) because attention and key-value (KV) caches grow with runtime, forcing either expensive inference or near-sighted sliding windows. We introduce CacheFlow, a training-free pipeline that pairs Dynamic Token Dropping (DTD) with a compressive long-term memory. DTD prunes per-patch tokens online via cosine similarity to the previous frame, and surviving tokens are packed into fixed-size blocks. This online, per-frame processing makes our approach fundamentally suited for live streaming VQA. As blocks are processed, each one's keys are summarized by a tiny recurrent encoder to form a retrieval index, while the block's full KV pairs are offloaded and later rehydrated for generation, preserving answer fidelity. At inference, a consensus-based retrieval mechanism retrieves only the Top-K most relevant blocks and attends over both the retrieved and local context for precise, long-range reasoning. CacheFlow is drop-in, architecture-agnostic, and requires no fine-tuning. Experiments on both offline and streaming VQA benchmarks demonstrate that CacheFlow outperforms current strong baselines, while processing up to 87% less tokens. Our dual approach enables VLMs to be both efficient and context-aware, paving the way for practical long-form video understanding.

2. S3-Attention:Attention-Aligned Endogenous Retrieval for Memory-Bounded Long-Context Inference (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Large language models are increasingly applied to multi-document and long-form inputs, yet long-context inference remains memory- and noise-inefficient. Key-value (KV) caching scales linearly with context length, while external retrieval methods often return lexically similar but causally irrelevant passages. We present S3-Attention, a memory-first inference-time framework that treats long-context processing as attention-aligned endogenous retrieval. S3-Attention decodes transient key and query projections into top-k sparse feature identifiers using lightweight sparse autoencoders, and constructs a CPU-based inverted index mapping features to token positions or spans during a single streaming scan. This design allows the KV cache to be discarded entirely and bounds GPU memory usage by the scan chunk size. At generation time, feature co-activation is used to retrieve compact evidence spans, optionally fused with BM25 for exact lexical matching. Under a unified LongBench evaluation protocol with fixed prompting, decoding, and matched token budgets, S3-Hybrid closely matches full-context inference across multiple model families and improves robustness in several information-dense settings. We also report an engineering limitation of the current prototype, which incurs higher wall-clock latency than optimized full-KV baselines, motivating future kernel-level optimization.

3. StreamMem: Query-Agnostic KV Cache Memory for Streaming Video Understanding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 28)
   Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made significant progress in visual-language reasoning, but their ability to efficiently handle long videos remains limited. Despite recent advances in long-context MLLMs, storing and attending to the key-value (KV) cache for long visual contexts incurs substantial memory and computational overhead. Existing visual compression methods require either encoding the entire visual context before compression or having access to the questions in advance, which is impractical for long video understanding and multi-turn conversational settings. In this work, we propose StreamMem, a query-agnostic KV cache memory mechanism for streaming video understanding. Specifically, StreamMem encodes new video frames in a streaming manner, compressing the KV cache using attention scores between visual tokens and generic query tokens, while maintaining a fixed-size KV memory to enable efficient question answering (QA) in memory-constrained, long-video scenarios. Evaluation on three long video understanding and two streaming video question answering benchmarks shows that StreamMem achieves state-of-the-art performance in query-agnostic KV cache compression and is competitive with query-aware compression approaches.

4. TokenSkipping: A Practical and Robust KV Cache Pruning Method for Long-Context LLM Inference (International Conference on Industrial Technology 2025, cites: 0)
   Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities, yet their deployment in long-context scenarios is severely hampered by the extensive memory and computational costs associated with the Key-Value (KV) cache. This paper introduces TokenSkipping, a practical and efficient KV cache pruning method designed to mitigate these challenges. TokenSkipping is a training-free, stateless, pre-storage method that employs a lightweight heuristic based on the L2 norm of key vectors, combined with a novel quota-based streaming algorithm for auto-regressive decoding. By deciding whether to admit or skip each token before writing to the cache, it eliminates cache-wide re-scoring and remains compatible with fused-attention kernels and Grouped Query Attention. A dual-mode design matches practical inference: during prefill, the policy retains the top-k highest-norm tokens under a fixed budget; during decoding, a streaming rule compares each new token’s norm to a reservoir-sampled percentile threshold, guaranteeing strict memory bounds with O(1) overhead. Anchor tokens are preserved to stabilize attention, and the policy operates independently per layer without retraining or kernel changes. Evaluated on Llama 3.1 8B (tested up to 128k tokens) and Mixtral-8 × 7B within their supported context windows, TokenSkipping substantially reduces KV cache memory while keeping throughput competitive with unpruned inference and cache-wide norm-based baselines. Our results reveal a distinct trade-off profile: aggressive pruning degrades fine-grained retrieval tasks (such as Needle-In-A-Haystack and QA), but TokenSkipping maintains robust performance on semantic summarization and coherence benchmarks, indicating that norm-based pruning can preserve global semantic context even when local precision is compressed. Compared to naive baselines like Sliding Window, TokenSkipping achieves better coherence under similar memory budgets and avoids global scoring, offering a simple, high-throughput optimization for memory-constrained long-context deployments.

5. ILRe: Intermediate Layer Retrieval for Context Compression in Causal Language Models (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated success across many benchmarks. However, they still exhibit limitations in long-context scenarios, primarily due to their short effective context length, quadratic computational complexity, and high memory overhead when processing lengthy inputs. To mitigate these issues, we introduce a novel context compression pipeline, called Intermediate Layer Retrieval (ILRe), which determines one intermediate decoder layer offline, encodes context by streaming chunked prefill only up to that layer, and recalls tokens by the attention scores between the input query and full key cache in that specified layer. In particular, we propose a multi-pooling kernels allocating strategy in the token recalling process to maintain the completeness of semantics. Our approach not only reduces the prefilling complexity from $O(L^2)$ to $O(L)$ and trims the memory footprint to a few tenths of that required for the full context, but also delivers performance comparable to or superior to the full-context setup in long-context scenarios. Without additional post training or operator development, ILRe can process a single $1M$ tokens request in less than half a minute (speedup $\approx 180\times$) and scores RULER-$1M$ benchmark of $\approx 79.8$ with model Llama-3.1-UltraLong-8B-1M-Instruct on a Huawei Ascend 910B NPU.

6. RELIC: Interactive Video World Model with Long-Horizon Memory (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 31)
   A truly interactive world model requires three key ingredients: real-time long-horizon streaming, consistent spatial memory, and precise user control. However, most existing approaches address only one of these aspects in isolation, as achieving all three simultaneously is highly challenging-for example, long-term memory mechanisms often degrade real-time performance. In this work, we present RELIC, a unified framework that tackles these three challenges altogether. Given a single image and a text description, RELIC enables memory-aware, long-duration exploration of arbitrary scenes in real time. Built upon recent autoregressive video-diffusion distillation techniques, our model represents long-horizon memory using highly compressed historical latent tokens encoded with both relative actions and absolute camera poses within the KV cache. This compact, camera-aware memory structure supports implicit 3D-consistent content retrieval and enforces long-term coherence with minimal computational overhead. In parallel, we fine-tune a bidirectional teacher video model to generate sequences beyond its original 5-second training horizon, and transform it into a causal student generator using a new memory-efficient self-forcing paradigm that enables full-context distillation over long-duration teacher as well as long student self-rollouts. Implemented as a 14B-parameter model and trained on a curated Unreal Engine-rendered dataset, RELIC achieves real-time generation at 16 FPS while demonstrating more accurate action following, more stable long-horizon streaming, and more robust spatial-memory retrieval compared with prior work. These capabilities establish RELIC as a strong foundation for the next generation of interactive world modeling.

7. FrameVGGT: Geometry-Aligned Frame-Level Memory for Bounded Streaming VGGT ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Streaming Visual Geometry Transformers such as StreamVGGT enable strong online 3D perception, but their KV-cache grows unbounded over long streams, limiting practical deployment. We revisit bounded-memory streaming from the perspective of geometric support. Unlike language modeling, where useful information can often be compressed at the token level, geometry-driven reasoning depends on redundant and mutually compatible multi-view support. Under fixed budgets, token-level retention can fragment within-frame evidence, weaken the coherence of geometric support, and make stable long-horizon inference more difficult. Motivated by this observation, we propose FrameVGGT, a bounded explicit-memory framework that organizes each frame's incremental KV contribution as a coherent frame-level segment. FrameVGGT summarizes each segment with a lightweight key-space prototype and maintains a fixed-capacity memory of complementary segments, with an optional sparse anchor tier for difficult long-horizon intervals. Across long-sequence 3D reconstruction, video depth estimation, and camera pose estimation, FrameVGGT achieves favorable accuracy-memory trade-offs under bounded memory while maintaining more stable geometry over long streams.

8. Stacked from One: Multi-Scale Self-Injection for Context Window Extension ( 2026, cites: 0)
   The limited context window of contemporary large language models (LLMs) remains a primary bottleneck for their broader application across diverse domains. Although continual pre-training on long-context data offers a straightforward solution, it incurs prohibitive data acquisition and computational costs. To address this challenge, we propose~\modelname, a novel framework based on multi-grained context compression and query-aware information acquisition. SharedLLM comprises two stacked short-context LLMs: a lower model serving as a compressor and an upper model acting as a decoder. The lower model compresses long inputs into compact, multi-grained representations, which are then forwarded to the upper model for context-aware processing. To maximize efficiency, this information transfer occurs exclusively at the lowest layers, bypassing lengthy forward passes and redundant cross-attention operations. This entire process, wherein the upper and lower models are derived from the same underlying LLM layers, is termed~\textit{self-injection}. To support this architecture, a specialized tree-based data structure enables the efficient encoding and query-aware retrieval of contextual information. Despite being trained on sequences of only 8K tokens, \modelname~effectively generalizes to inputs exceeding 128K tokens. Across a comprehensive suite of long-context modeling and understanding benchmarks, \modelname~achieves performance superior or comparable to strong baselines, striking an optimal balance between efficiency and accuracy. Furthermore, these design choices allow \modelname~to substantially reduce the memory footprint and yield notable inference speedups ($2\times$ over streaming and $3\times$ over encoder-decoder architectures).

9. InternLM-XComposer2.5-OmniLive: A Comprehensive Multimodal System for Long-term Streaming Video and Audio Interactions (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 42)
   Creating AI systems that can interact with environments over long periods, similar to human cognition, has been a longstanding research goal. Recent advancements in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made significant strides in open-world understanding. However, the challenge of continuous and simultaneous streaming perception, memory, and reasoning remains largely unexplored. Current MLLMs are constrained by their sequence-to-sequence architecture, which limits their ability to process inputs and generate responses simultaneously, akin to being unable to think while perceiving. Furthermore, relying on long contexts to store historical data is impractical for long-term interactions, as retaining all information becomes costly and inefficient. Therefore, rather than relying on a single foundation model to perform all functions, this project draws inspiration from the concept of the Specialized Generalist AI and introduces disentangled streaming perception, reasoning, and memory mechanisms, enabling real-time interaction with streaming video and audio input. The proposed framework InternLM-XComposer2.5-OmniLive (IXC2.5-OL) consists of three key modules: (1) Streaming Perception Module: Processes multimodal information in real-time, storing key details in memory and triggering reasoning in response to user queries. (2) Multi-modal Long Memory Module: Integrates short-term and long-term memory, compressing short-term memories into long-term ones for efficient retrieval and improved accuracy. (3) Reasoning Module: Responds to queries and executes reasoning tasks, coordinating with the perception and memory modules. This project simulates human-like cognition, enabling multimodal large language models to provide continuous and adaptive service over time.

10. Memo: Training Memory-Efficient Embodied Agents with Reinforcement Learning (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 4)

In the current paradigm of training transformer-based policies for embodied sequential decision-making tasks, visual inputs often overwhelm the context limits of transformers, while humans can maintain and utilize a lifetime of experience compressed as memories. Significant compression is possible in principle, as much of the input is irrelevant and can be abstracted. However, existing approaches predominantly focus on either recurrent models with fixed-size memory or transformers with full-context reliance. In this work, we propose Memo, a transformer-based architecture and training recipe for reinforcement learning (RL) on memory-intensive, long-horizon tasks. Memo incorporates the creation and retrieval of memory by interleaving periodic summarization tokens with the inputs of a model during training. We demonstrate Memo's effectiveness on a gridworld meta-RL benchmark and a multi-object navigation task in photo-realistic indoor settings. Memo outperforms naive long-context transformer baselines while being more compute and storage efficient. Additionally, Memo generalizes better to longer contexts at inference time and remains robust in streaming settings, where historical context must be truncated to fit inference constraints. Our code is available at: https://github.com/gunshi/memo.

Ablation studies highlight the contributions of each component, confirming the effectiveness of our approach. Based on this observation, we utilize a novel tree-search-like algorithm that estimates the top-$k$ key tokens for a given query on the fly, which is mathematically guaranteed to have better performance than random attention pruning. Although near-oracle top-k KV selection can preserve the quality of dense attention while sharply reducing computation and bandwidth, existing sparse methods generally rely on posterior heuristics, i.e., selectors conditioned on observed attention or proxy scores. Such conditioning introduces posterior bias: it tends to distort true token importance and miss salient tokens, thereby impairing long-range reasoning. To tackle this problem, we propose Pre-hoc Sparsity (PrHS), which selects KV entries before attention scoring and provides explicit accuracy control. Let the attention mass of discarded entries be delta (the dropped mass). Through a marginal-to-mutual-information analysis, we derive an upper bound on the mutual-information loss that depends only on the dropped mass. This relation explains failure modes of posterior heuristics and enables verifiable guarantees by controlling the dropped mass in advance. Within PrHS, we instantiate three orthogonal pre-hoc selectors along the axes of time, depth, and layer. Extensive experiments on LLaMA and Mistral families validate PrHS. Across GSM8K and CoQA, PrHS reduces retrieval overhead by over 90%, achieving 3x higher retrieval sparsity than HShare at matched or better accuracy. It incurs under 1% average degradation on LongBench, lowers attention FLOPs by about 15% versus prior sparse baselines, and yields a 9.9x speedup in attention-operator latency and 2.8x higher throughput on NVIDIA A100-80GB GPUs than the dense baseline.

This approach enhances the precision of chunk selection across both in-domain and out-of-domain context lengths. To make HSA efficient, we further introduce a hardware-aligned kernel design. By combining HSA with Mamba, we introduce RAMba, which achieves perfect accuracy in passkey retrieval across 64 million contexts despite pre-training on only 4K-length contexts, and significant improvements on various downstream tasks, with nearly constant memory footprint. Qualitative analyses further reveal that learned retention scores align with human intuition, naturally recovering heuristics such as sink tokens, sliding windows, and gist compression without explicit design. Beyond efficiency, retention scores provide insights into layer- and head-specific roles, suggesting a new path toward LLM interpretability.
